desc:
The heavy forest to the west gives way to lighter woodland here. The trees seem
to filter the light, giving the forest a soft glow. Single mallorn-trees stand
here and there in islands of light; they appear to be more prevalent to the
north and east. By sunlight or moonlight they magnify the light, making the
woods as bright as if you were on an open plain. The grasses between the trees
are grazed short to form natural lawns.
name: Mixed Woods

desc:
The woodland here is enchanting. The mallorn-trees seem to capture spring,
summer and autumn all at once, with many in blossom and a gentle rain of leaves
and petals in the almost undetectable breeze. Each tree stands separate from the
others, forming a matrix of natural avenues. Not all the trees are mellyrn, but
those that are not seem to be enchanted with the same spell, following the
discipline of the mallorn-trees. The light here is soft, with the golden leaves
glistening in the higher branches. You hear the murmur of the river a short
distance to the east.
name: Golden Woodland

desc:
The river turns from the west to the south. The flow is strong and the river is
wide yet silent. The eastern bank is high and nearly vertical; a narrow ledge
near its bottom might serve as a safe, if none too dry, landing for a small
boat. Bright pine trees cover the top of the eastern bank, while the northern
bank is higher and allows no glimpse of what lies on its top. The forest to the
southwest is bright with fresh colours of green, white and gold. You catch the
odd glimpse of golden and white trees; the latter must be in blossom.
name: Bend in the Anduin

desc:
Just to the west the land drops abruptly to where a large river flows many feet
below. From the edge of the steep bank the ground rises steadily to the
northwest. Bright green pine trees cling precariously to the slope, perhaps only
just winning the constant battle with gravity. The ground has obviously slipped
recently, leaving a great scar of mud in the slopes that elsewhere are covered
with pine needles. Near the riverbank you can see several dead trees.
name: Forested Slope

desc:
Tall pine trees stand on the top of this high bank. They are widely spaced and
the ground between them is crossed with the lattice of their roots in the
shallow soil. Fallen trees can be seen here and there; large flower-shaped fungi
grow in the rotting wood. Far below to the south you can hear the roar of the
great river scouring its way through its rocky passage. The trees continue in
all other directions.
name: Pine Forest

desc:
Under the shadow of the forest to the east, this patch of grassland is less
exposed than the land to the west. Grass and other plants hold the soft earth
together. To the east the ground is water and mud, making progress in that
direction impossible. A small trail or animal track leads north-south helping to
pick out firmer footholds. Three small circles of stones show that someone
camped here but it is not clear how long ago this happened.
name: Wet Grassland

desc:
A natural basin filled with clear water lies here amidst the rolling green
meadows. The lush grasses of the meadows are interspersed with marsh marigolds
and other colourful flowers. In the lowest spots small dark pools of water stand
surrounded by sphagnum moss. Strong smells of herbs penetrate the air.
name: Wetland Meadows

desc:
The gently rolling plains are covered by a varied tapestry of rich greens, golds
and browns. Here and there you spot the bright pink of a tall thistle or the
yellow of a marsh marigold. The grasslands continue in a similar vein in all
directions with a general incline to the south where the ground appears to be
retaining water and softening. In the softer spots water fills footprints as
soon as they appear.
name: Rich Grasslands

desc:
The rolling landscape is formed by many low hills that are slightly elevated
above the plains to the north and east and continue to rise to the west and
south. Between the hills are a few narrow, well-defined valleys with gurgling
streams tumbling east. Here and there one can see a spring emerging from a
hillside. Some of the taller hills are capped with crags which look as though
they may harbour caves.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
A low hill or natural mound with a flat top rises here. The ground around it is
little more than bare rock. Wind and rain have created small cracks in the rock
where enough soil has gathered to allow a few short tufts of grass to take root.
The ground to the west drops quickly, forming a small plain, and then rises
again to form a small group of hillocks. A low mound stands at some distance to
the north, and a small rocky outcropping rises to the northwest. To the east the
ground slopes away to the rolling grasslands below.
name: A Small Hill

desc:
Low hills surround this small plain covered by long dry grasses. The rocky
ground to the north rises more gently than the hills to the east. The plain
continues into the horizon to the south; on a bright day around noon it would be
an almost blinding suntrap. The grasses rustle in the wind and when disturbed by
small birds and animals.
name: Grassy Plain between the Hills

desc:
Here the trail, which is no larger than a rough animal track, picks its way
through the hills. The gradients become steeper to the south and far to the
southwest you can see the greys and blues of the mountains. To the south, as the
ground continues to rise, the vegetation prevalent in the grasslands gives way
to heather-covered moors interspersed with sparsely covered heathland. The trail
passes through a few high points where one can catch glimpses of the track as it
continues north and south. Here and there you see outcrops of large rocks which
would allow you some protection from the elements if you were to camp.
name: Trail through the Low Hills

desc:
The purple, brown and green carpet of heather ripples in the wind on this
exposed heath. A crag the height of three men stands above you. Some cracks in
the rock might allow you to climb up. You see the remains of a small campfire at
its base on the flat rock. Some water has collected in the small bowls eroded in
the granite by aeons of harsh winds.
name: Heath below a Crag

desc:
From the top of a hillock in these scantly covered grasslands you are able to
see a faint north-south trail picking its way through the rolling hills to your
east. Far beyond the trail the grey shadow of a narrow band of woodland picks
out the course of a great river. The only features breaking up the monotony to
the west are a few rocky outcrops; a clump of trees huddled together is to your
north.
name: Bare Grasslands

desc:
Large rocky formations rise here and there, making passage difficult. Heaps of
loose debris cover the ground and some bigger boulders lie about; these rocks
have a rough, scratched surface, and they stand a bit slanted, as if leaning
against the wind. Nothing grows here except some stunted grass which tries to
survive amidst the rocky debris.
name: Rocky Terrain

desc:
Large boulders lie scattered around here and the landscape is barren and bleak.
Some grass grows here in clumps, but mostly the terrain is dominated by big
stony formations. While the surrounding landscape is fairly level, the ground
here is rough and passage more difficult. Southwards the rocky plains seem to
continue, but in other directions the grasslands reign.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
A half dozen leafless trees here do little to shelter you from the winds blowing
across the plains. Yet from this shallow basin you are unable to see much of the
surrounding terrain other than hills to the south. The earth is soft, wet,
smells of peat and is perhaps not firm enough to support your weight. With your
every movement water fills in the footprints. Glancing at these little pools,
you see they are teeming with small water insects.
name: Clump of Trees

desc:
The brown and gold of the bracken on these slight slopes contrasts with the
greens and lighter browns of the grasslands to the north and the darker shades
of the heath to the south. This vegetation crunches under foot, sounding
unnaturally loud in the silence. The horizon to the north and west is filled
with the light grey shadow of a forest.
name: Bracken Covered Slope

desc:
A faint trail crosses the rolling plains in the north, winds through
boulder-strewn fields and climbs into the low hills in the south. Short grasses
cover the ground; here and there tufts of longer grass or thistles wave in the
breeze. A few small cool streams cross the trail and run away to the southeast.
The grassland flattens to the northwest and undulating plains stretch out to the
east.
name: Faint Trail

desc:
In the vast fields of tall grass stand a half dozen forms made of hard stone,
defying the elements. The sand carried by the winds has worn away the softer
stone in this outcropping. Small cracks in the stone have trapped some soil and
water that nurtures a few flowering mountain plants. The ground rises to the
south; to the west you are able to make out a fine north-south trail. The
blue-grey shadow of a forest looms on the horizon to the northeast.
name: Granite Spires

desc:
An ancient burial mound sits here, looking somewhat out of place in the
otherwise featureless grass-covered moorlands. The ash-coloured sides of the
mound are devoid of vegetation and look unnatural. On one of the sides a few
rocks have been arranged into a strange pattern. From the top of the mound in
good light one can make out the odd ruined building or cairn which catches the
sun. An outcrop of rocks rises to the west. In all other directions the
grasslands roll away from you.
name: Burial Mound

desc:
Due to unstable soil and the shifting rock strata, a giant sinkhole has formed
in the ground here. Small bushes and trees cling to its steep sides which
shelter them from the winds. Some large rocks are haphazardly arranged at the
bottom. The shape of the sinkhole causes the wind to swirl around it; over many
years, the wind and the water have eroded many cracks in the rocks below. A
faint track leads directly down at an alarming angle.
name: Above a Sinkhole

desc:
The grasslands extend in all directions as far as the eye can see. In the north
the monotony is broken by the silhouette of a small mound and the dark shadow of
a forest on the horizon beyond the mound. Here and there small streams cross the
land in knee-deep cuts. The dark water of the streams seems to have been stained
by the local soils.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
The undulating fields are covered by thick green grass. Looking westwards, this
featureless land gives you little sense of distance to measure your progress.
The general lie of the land appears to fall to the southeast. Close to the east
there is a large wood, the edge of which curves round to the north.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
Two tall trees stand like a pair of great pillars in this clearing near the
eaves of the forest. They are heavily charred, having been struck by lightning
which seems to have ended their ancient reign. To the west you glimpse rolling
grasslands through the thin belt of trees. The area around the pair of trees
seems to have been used for a camp, perhaps for a large host, but not since the
last rain.
name: Burnt Trees

desc:
The forest here has petered out into a mixture of scrub and brushland. This
vegetation gives some cover from prying eyes. A gentle slope to the west rolls
into the grasslands which stretch endlessly into the distance. Judging by the
many burrows among the tree roots, a host of small animals have made this area
their home. Bright marsh flowers grow here and there, thriving in the moist
soil. A few tiny streams run eastward.
name: Forest Edge

desc:
Thin wiry bushes cover this gentle slope that leads into the forest in the north
and east. The clay ground holds a lot of water making passage a little tricky. A
small hillock rises to the west; on its top you can just make out a small pile
of stones. The ground falls to the south and east where marsh flowers and small
bushes grow among the grasses with flat leaves.
name: Marshy Ground

desc:
A small pile of stones stands on the top of a low hillock as the sole marker of
a forlorn grave of a deceased warrior or traveller. A solitary dead tree is
visible at some distance to the north. Beyond it, the darker wall of the forest
looms on the horizon. The ground slopes down gently to the southeast where it
gradually becomes softer. A few flowering plants and small shrubs dot the even
grassland; these seem to grow more frequent to the east.
name: Small Cairn

desc:
These fields of long grass are fairly unremarkable, with no distinct landmarks
save the odd stripe of greener grass covering long unused thoroughfares and the
curtain of the forest rising to the north. Many small insects jump around in the
grass or climb the tall stems. The ground slopes down gently to the south and
then rises again in the distance before rolling away in a series of glacial
folds.
name: Grassy Fields

desc:
This is the very edge of a large forest. The trees thin out toward the south.
There appears to be a change in soil conditions to the south, accounting for
different vegetation. The ground holds more water there and in places becomes
marshy. Low marsh plants and wiry bushes gradually replace the trees, most of
which are dead and rotting.
name: Eaves of the Forest

desc:
The bank skirts the bend of the river that flows to the northeast. Trees
overhang the water, seemingly pleased with being able to catch more light. The
trees are mostly grey and bear few leaves in spite of all the sunlight they get.
Farther from the riverbank the forest floor is carpeted with small dense shrubs.
A fallen tree at the water's edge has provided a place for beavers to create a
little pool of water by building a small dam. A few leaves and insects on the
water show a gentle back-eddy. Here and there you spot the tracks of a small
bird or animal.
name: Tree-lined Bank

desc:
The closely standing trees and the thick undergrowth form a dense barrier
between the river and the large expanse of forest to the west. The clay bank is
high and slippery but is held together by a tangle of roots and boulders. There
is no suitable place to land a boat. The water below the bank foams as it rushes
through channels formed by large rocks, in places creating a spray of white
water.
name: Thick Woods at the Edge of the River

desc:
The trees here are old and carry an air of nobility. Although they show clear
signs of age, they seem well preserved as if by some magic; yet perhaps now is
the eve of their life. The sound of a river rushing through the rapids can be
heard in the east. To the north you can see a dilapidated wooden structure. To
the south the vegetation between the trees grows thicker.
name: Light Forest

desc:
The collapsed remains of a large boathouse are perched on the bank above a bend
in the river. The walls of the structure are wooden, but the slipway is
constructed of neat stone blocks. The slipway is now covered with debris but
could still be serviceable. Moss, lichen and fungi grow on the wooden parts of
the ruined building. Golden crowns of the mellyrn light up the woodland to the
west, but the forest to the south is bleak and silent.
name: Ruined Boathouse

desc:
The river here is slightly wider and slower than it is both upstream and
downstream as it flows between two bends on a gentle gradient. To the north the
bank is a sheer cliff. Slightly above the water level a narrow ledge juts out
from the northern bank. The whispers of the woodlands to the south are
complemented by the rumbling sound of distant rapids.
name: River Anduin

desc:
The river turns from the west to the south here. The water is white in places
where it crashes against large rocks. These rocks must have been carried down
the river during times of high flooding, caused by the melting of ice and snow
during the short summers seen high in the Misty Mountains. The green woods to
the north of the river stand on top of a sheer bank, but to the east the bank is
not so steep and the trees climb down to the very edge of the water.
name: Bend in the Anduin

desc:
Here the River Anduin is strong and swift. The banks grow steeper from the north
to the south. A rumbling sound of rapids comes from the south. Thick woods stand
on the high banks, forming an avenue where the passage of the river could easily
be watched. There are a few spots on the west bank where one might be able to
scramble ashore.
name: Above the Rapids

desc:
Jammed by steep banks on both sides, the river here is in turmoil. Large rocks
in the middle of the river create dangerous rapids. There seems to be no way of
leaving the river here and the strength of the water prevents you from going
upstream. Forests stand on the high banks making this stretch of water cool and
sunless.
name: Rapids in the River Anduin

desc:
The river turns from the north to the east here. Skirting the woodlands to the
west and south, it hurries forward on its quest to find the sea. The water is
white in places where great rocks channel it into short rapids. Several small
streams tumble down to the river from the forest above.
name: Bend in the River

desc:
Old, grey trees grow on the very edge of the river that makes a bend to the
north. The bank is high and steep, with many overhangs where river creatures
could lurk. In a few spots forest streams cut through the bank, washing their
way through the sand and clay. At these openings the trees dip their roots into
the river below, creating natural jetties. Water swirls through them, creating
back currents that could be dangerous for a careless swimmer.
name: Ancient Trees on the Riverbank

desc:
This forest between the river to the northeast and the plain to the southwest is
a mixture of oak, elm and ash. The trees nearer the river look unhealthy; many
of them are dead and rotting, apparently due to the flooding caused by several
severe winters. To the north the trees grow thicker.
name: Light Woods

desc:
A ruined stone building stands here. The forest has largely overgrown it and
there is little left of the roof or the top portion of the walls. From the top
of the ruins one can see beyond the low bushes to the south. The view in that
direction is that of grassy rolling plains with a small hillock. A small pile of
stones sits on the top of the hillock; it is the only distinctive feature in the
otherwise bleak scene.
name: Ruined Farm House

desc:
The forest here is so dense that virtually no light penetrates the canopy of
branches. Many beards of moss hang from the trees, blocking the light. Large
masses of mud and branches in the crowns of the trees are also visible in the
gloom. The vegetation on the ground is scrappy and leafless, but there are many
thorny bushes that appear to thrive in the dark.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
The trees here are closely packed. Most of them are tall, thin and bear few
leaves. Thorny bushes and fallen branches make passage through the forest slow
and painful. You hear the occasional crack of a dry branch and the sounds of
small animals or birds picking their way through the brambles.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
The forest here is still, silent and airless. The trees are grey and bear few
leaves. Dry leaves, rotten branches and large sheets of bark litter the ground.
On the tree-boles there are many bare patches covered in a pepper of white
fungus. Many large spider webs are strung between the branches; in some of them
you spot cocooned corpses of small animals. In all directions except to the
south the forest seems to grow lighter, but to the south it gets even duller and
darker.
name: Diseased Forest

desc:
The trees here are young, healthy and bright with many blossoms; here and there
you can spot the golden crowns of the mellyrn. On one of the wooded lawns a lone
dead mallorn attracts the eye. The chattering of birds and other woodland
creatures seems to have stopped around it. To the south the trees darken, but in
the west they grow lighter although the forest does not seem to grow less dense.
To the north the ground slopes down to the river as it does to the east, where
one can see the remains of a wooden building.
name: Dead Mallorn

desc:
The river turns from the north to the east, widening a little before continuing
east. The water is white with foam in the spots where it meets small rocks or
sharp rock edges but calms as the river turns. The northeast bank is high and
rises sharply. The woods to the south and west are bright; some of the trees are
in blossom and have a distinct feel of springtime.
name: Bend in the River

desc:
A river runs through the forest to the east. The trees by the riverbank are
bright with golden leaves, silvery bark and occasional blossoms; their branches
bend like those of the weeping willows. The trees at the water's edge dip the
tips of their branches into the water. A carpet of golden leaves lines the
natural avenues formed by the rows of trees. The forest to the south darkens a
bit.
name: Golden Trees

desc:
The trees here are young and bright. Many are in blossom and perhaps one in five
is a golden mallorn. A light, refreshing breeze moves the tree branches. From
the northeast you hear many odd noises similar to the sounds made by water birds
and frogs. Small white mushrooms grow on the grass lawns between the trees.
name: Tree-covered Lawns

desc:
The mixed trees here are mature and well spaced as if they had been planted this
way on purpose. They exhibit a tapestry of colours - several shades of green,
brown, orange and gold, with white pink and yellow blossoms. A small path leads
north; as your eye follows it you catch a glimpse of a golden mallorn-tree
gleaming in the soft light. The trees to the south grow dense and darken a
little.
name: Light Woods

desc:
An ancient oak tree lies here. From its condition it is apparent that it was
brought down by high winds and lightning. Many of the top branches have been
trimmed with neat axe strokes. The wood is still green and the only growth on
the trunk is the moss on what was the southern face. Several of the surrounding
trees appear to have been damaged by the fall.
name: Fallen Oak

desc:
The ancient oaks stand well apart and there is little vegetation between them,
which allows light to trickle down through the leaves all the way to the ground.
The leaves of the oaks rustle as if squirrels were scampering among the
branches. Birds chatter in the trees-tops; now and then, the woods are disturbed
by a howl of a wolf.
name: Oak Forest

desc:
The tall oak trees here are old and strong. Between the trees, many stumps are
rotting in the ground. Judging by the axe marks on the stumps, these trees were
probably felled for timber. The remains of a small camp are visible amid the
grass; the campsite looks several weeks old. A few barely discernible cart
tracks lead south, disappearing in the featureless grasslands.
name: Forest Edge

desc:
The grasses on these south-facing slopes are interspersed with small flowing
plants and thistles. From the shape of the land it appears that this area might
have once been farmed. There are various tracks on the ground; most have been
made by mounts, but several deep cuts in the earth seem to have been caused by
the dragging of heavy objects.
name: Fields near the Forest

desc:
The mixed trees thin out rapidly to the south and west. Many have been felled,
leaving rotting stumps and branches strewn carelessly on the forest floor. You
can clearly see the marks where the trunks have been dragged away. Other
remains, including small circles of ashes from campfires, show that it has been
a few weeks since the last activity in this area.
name: Forest Edge

desc:
The trees here are tall and thin, bearing almost no leaves on their long
branches. The occasional leaves and bark are covered with a fine dusting of
parasitic growth. Large slugs and snails cluster in the root lattices; tracks of
their sticky residue leave a silvery film, further contributing to the lack of
colour in the area. The forest continues in all directions.
name: Grey Forest

desc:
The majority of trees here are old and gnarled. Great fronds of fungal flesh
cling to them. While none of the trees appear to be dead, there seems to be a
continuous struggle as if the forest was touched by evil. To the north and east
the trees thicken, their bright green leaves indicating the healthier parts of
the forest. The trees to the south are dark and grey with few leaves on their
branches. You hear the occasional cautious call of a lone bird. To the west the
trees thin out.
name: Ageing Wood

desc:
The forest here seems to exhibit the signs of all the seasons simultaneously.
Some trees are showing new leaves, some are in blossom, others are bearing fruit
and a few are shedding leaves. You hear the gentle trickle of a spring to the
east, the singing of birds and the chatter of small tree-dwelling animals. The
ground between the trees is covered by a golden carpet of leaves.
name: Enchanted Woods

desc:
Here the trees are widely spaced, creating natural avenues. Single mallorn trees
stand in islands of light. Your eye is drawn to their silvery-grey bark and
golden crowns. Many of the other trees are in blossom or have bright green
leaves of spring growth. The ground is covered with lawns of soft grass and
carpets of golden leaves. Here and there small white flowers grow in patches
like a light sprinkling of snow.
name: Avenue of Trees in Blossom

desc:
A patchwork of greens, browns and golds greets your eyes in this part of the
forest. The frequency of mellyrn increases to the north and east. A small stream
runs east-west, and alongside it a small track winds its way among the roots.
The forest continues in all directions. Just to the west the ground rises
steeply.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
A small grove of ancient elm trees marks the edge of the forest that continues
to the east and north. The trees stand well apart; between them are tracts of
bright green grass and a few weathered boulders. The ancient trees appear to be
home to a variety of large fungi. These saprophytes look nearly as old as the
trees, and seem to do them no harm. The soil is soft and dark and pocked by the
feet of many grazing animals. The air smells damp and is unusually still. Now
and then you hear the odd snort of an ox or cow.
name: Ancient Grove

desc:
The ground here is sodden and sports only a few tufts of grass amid the pools of
stagnant water. Trees surround the swampy area on three sides with grasslands
stretching out to the west. The position of any traveller in this terrain would
be strategically weak, as it is impossible to move quickly and one could easily
be surprised by any would be attackers from the forest a bow's shot away.
name: Swampy Ground

desc:
This small group of trees stands about a hundred paces from the large forest to
the east. The ground around is soft and wet, which accounts for the absence of
trees immediately to the east and to the north. In the branches of one of the
trees someone has strung up a few ropes that form what appears to be a makeshift
bed. There are no other signs of inhabitance.
name: Small Copse

desc:
This south-facing field is dark and furrowed. It steams, gently releasing the
heat it has absorbed during the day. While you can see the great hoof-prints of
a large workhorse, there is no other sign of a farmer. The smell of the earth is
pleasant, as no manure has been used. Trees to the north and east could easily
conceal onlookers, but you hear nothing but a gentle breeze in branches.
name: Ploughed Field

desc:
The ground dips slightly, forming a small depression in which thick grass grows
over three feet high. Animal droppings, bones and tracks indicate that this area
conceals a variety of small animals and perhaps larger predators. To the west
you can see a strip of shorter grass of a lighter colour; this might be an
animal track. Beyond that strip, there is little to break up the rolling
grasslands. You are unable to see much of the terrain in other directions due to
the elevation and the amount of vegetation, but you can detect the scent of the
forest carried on the north-easterly breeze.
name: Tall Grass

desc:
The trail here is marked only by different length and pattern of grass. It
cannot have seen much heavy use recently. Tall grass covers the plains to the
east and west. The trail to the north bends westwards. To the south the land
begins to rise.
name: Light Trail

desc:
Here an ill-defined trail through the wild grasses turns west and south. The
forest threatens to invade the trail in places, striking from the northeast. To
the east between the trail and the trees lies a ploughed field. The ground to
the north is riddled with mole hills and rabbit holes showing the
russet-coloured soil. You hear the occasional chatter of birds.
name: Bend in the Light Trail

desc:
This bleak, fairly lifeless desolation continues in all directions. Farther to
the south the terrain becomes rougher and some dark, rocky shapes can be seen
rising in the distance. Here nothing but some loose stones lie around the
dull-coloured dusty ground.
name: Desolate Plains

desc:
The lands here are bare and lifeless with only the odd tuft of grass or thistle
waving in the wind. The terrain gently rises to the south, where the grasslands
become more undulating. The breeze carries a light smell of salt which seems to
account for the lack of vegetation. At low points in the landscape the horizon
is limited to the surrounding grasses; on the high points one could see little
to break the unchanging plains other than the hazy shadow of a large forest far
to the northwest.
name: Gently Rolling Plains

desc:
These plains are covered by tall dry grasses that rustle in the wind. The land
to the west seems bare and lifeless. The hills to the south exhibit a variety of
plant cover judging by the shades of blues and greys visible in the distance.
Just to the north a fine trail bends east and then curves southwards.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Here the ill-defined trail through the wild grasses turns east and north. The
fields to the west seem to indicate some previous habitation of the area,
whereas the grasslands to the southeast are the product of poor soils and
weathering. A forest dominates your view to the east. The trees nearest to you
are leafless and silent.
name: Bend in the Trail

desc:
You can see from the remains of several rows of fencing and dry stone walls that
these fields were once used for agricultural purposes. A square of stones marks
the place where a farmhouse once stood. To your east you spot the thin line of a
trail through barren lands. To the south the ground begins to rise and roll a
little.
name: Abandoned Fields

desc:
Once a fertile area, this land is now completely barren with nothing but short
grass populating the widespread fields. The open plains are spacious, but empty;
rarely does a traveller disturb the peace of these lands, and few animals can be
seen.
name: Ruined Fields

desc:
Tall and proud, the trees here are ancient even by elven reckoning. Stepping
into this forest amidst the wise silver trunks and softly swaying undergrowth it
seems that you have entered a land free from the grasp of time. The majestic,
golden-crowned mellyrn do not merely recall the Elder Days, but rather dwell in
them still. To the north the woods grow impossibly dense, dazzling and confusing
in their novel shape and colour. The occasional snowy-barked tree provides a
stunning contrast to the vast sea of mellyrn.
name: Mallorn Forest

desc:
The trees here are tall and tightly packed, limiting visibility in all
directions. Oak, beech and other deciduous hardwoods intermix with several
varieties of evergreen trees, which have liberally layered the hard dirt floor
with a prickly carpet of dark green needles. Most notably the occasional gold
and silver mallorn-tree sprouts from a small clearing in the forest, towering
high above the less ancient and majestic plant-life. One of these mallorn-trees
is exceptionally tall; its great crown rises high above the surrounding forest.
The faint sound of running water is barely detectable from the northeast.
name: Mallorn-spotted Woodland

desc:
A glade of silver-barked mallorn-trees rises high upon the banks of the crystal
clear Anduin, which flows away to the east. Each mallorn has a single, smooth
trunk which gives way to a golden crown supported by broad, upswept limbs not
unlike those of the beech. At ground level small snowdrop flowers lie scattered
across the soft turf as if a brief flurry of florae had passed through. A
single, white-barked tree grows in this woodland, beneath which lies a natural
garden of pale, bell-shaped and tall, star-silver flowers.
name: Majestic Forest

desc:
This forest is inhabited exclusively by the ancient mellyrn native to
Lothlorien. In contrast to the woodlands lying south and west, the wide-trunked
trees here are well spaced, leaving room for flowers of myriad colours to
flourish in the golden shade. To the north and east a wide, white-capped river
flows swiftly away to the south; farther in this direction a heavily wooded
ridge rises from water level and follows the Anduin's progress. Fair sounds of
laughter and sweet song seem to drift by occasionally, but perhaps they are only
illusions produced by the river's steady hum.
name: Mallorn Forest

desc:
This wooded rise protrudes into the wide, swift waters of the Anduin, which wrap
around from north to east. The forest continues to the west, following an upward
slope which culminates in lofty glades of a golden hue. Across the river in the
northeast a wooded ridge runs for several miles on a north-south axis.
name: Forest above the Anduin

desc:
The river flows deeply and swiftly between tree-topped riverbanks, emitting a
smooth, low pitched murmur as it bends from north to east. Healthy green
woodlands rise on the eastern and western shores; however their splendour is
dwarfed by the magnificent golden forest visible to the south and far away to
the west.
name: Deep and Flowing River

desc:
These waters are wide and extremely clear, yet it would be difficult for even
the most acute eyes to detect the rocky riverbed which lies far below the smooth
surface. Massive grey boulders, rounded smooth by the flow through countless
centuries, lie scattered and submerged near the western bank, as if mindlessly
dropped there by some earth-hewing immortal of old. The crisp water flows over
these boulders silently, creating dynamic arcs of blue and white.
name: South Flowing River

desc:
A narrow, dark grotto opens into the rocky depths of the stone face. Water
filters in through countless crevices in the western wall in a slow but steady
ebb and flow as on a tiny beach, but one with a shore of bare rock. The very
waters of the Anduin seem to have carved the cavern whimsically throughout the
years, forming angular walls that look like scarred faces. The ceiling is dotted
with stalactites like outstretched fingers trying to touch the waters that flood
the cave. The atmosphere is cool and very wet, and yet more pleasant than one
would expect.
name: Half-flooded Cavern

desc:
Narrow and uneven, a natural passageway opens its way through the rock. It is
somehow connected to the Great River and totally flooded with its cold waters.
These do not remain utterly still; one can notice slight currents running in
both directions. The tunnel takes a sharp bend here. Muffled echoes and a dim
glimpse of light reach faintly from above. Northward the passage continues,
shrouded in the deepest blackness.
name: An Underwater Passage

desc:
Even in its broadest spots the tunnel is no more than a couple yards wide. At
some places the walls of bare rock seem to stretch inward in sharp-edged
projections, as if trying to hinder your passage, although never actually
blocking it completely. The passage is plunged into utter darkness, but one can
grope ones way along the enclosing walls. The narrow tunnel continues southward
at the same level, but to the north it apparently turns upward.
name: A Water-filled Passage

desc:
You find yourself in a recondite grotto in the depths of the rocky ground. No
sort of natural light can ever reach this secluded cave that lies shrouded in
the thickest shadows. A small lake takes up most of the room in the cavern. Its
waters, black like a moonless night, hardly show any sign of motion, as if
frozen, or as though time had stopped long ago in this forsaken cave. At the far
end of the cave, to the west, you can catch a glimpse of a dry corner rising
slightly above the waters.
name: A Small Subterranean Lake

desc:
In the darkest corner of this forgotten grotto, where the deep shadows have
never been disturbed by the sunlight, lies a small, roughly level area, like a
rocky beach descending gently into the silent lakeshore. Its stony ground is
uneven and dangerously slippery. Here and there heaps of fishbones lie
carelessly piled, mixed with other filth of dubious origin. Both muck and
moisture contribute to the piercing stench that fills the air. But even in this
lost spot, as in the wilderness outside, the atmosphere remains mild no matter
the season, perhaps under the influence of the same strange power.
name: At the Back of the Cavern

desc:
A narrow ledge juts out of the high, sheer cliff wall that forms the riverbank.
Unable to break the resistance of the cliff, the river makes a turn along its
side, its waters washing over the ledge that lies just above the water level.
Crests of foam spring up on the waves that break against the protruding edges in
the rock. The ledge is slightly over four feet wide and could serve as a landing
spot for a small craft.
name: Below the Cliff Wall

desc:
A wooded hillock rises above the banks of the wide Anduin, which wraps around
these lands from west to south. The trees here have slender trunks but are lofty
in height and seem to become closer together in the north. Beyond the western
banks the river flows powerfully yet quietly over several large, rounded
boulders scattered throughout the waterway.
name: Banks of the Anduin

desc:
Slender birch trees weave their limbs overhead, providing an airy ceiling to
these woodlands. In sharp contrast to the deciduous trees which can be seen in
all other directions, the forest in the south is dominated by long-needled pine
trees. The faint sounds of a river seem to drift in fleetingly from the west,
but they are too vague to be definitively distinguished.
name: Amidst the Birch Trees

desc:
The wide, smooth and silver-barked trunk of this mallorn-tree rises over four
yards above ground level before several sturdy yet flexible boughs branch off it
at an upswept angle. Upon the great branches, which sway smoothly in the passing
wind, a small, unobtrusive platform of slender grey planks has been constructed.
A hole has been cut in the middle of the floor, next to which has been secured a
finely crafted rope ladder. The platform has no walls or ceiling, but rather is
enclosed in all directions by glittering golden masses of the broad mallorn
leaves, supported by streaks of silver boughs. It is doubtful that even the most
keen-eyed passer-by would be able to detect this structure, which blends
seamlessly into its woody surroundings.
name: Upon the Silver Boughs

desc:
This flet is actually constructed of several irregularly shaped wooden
platforms, interconnected by the broad silver boughs of the great mallorn-tree.
The individual platforms have been designed to exist in harmony with this
golden-crowned wonder; each structure is shaped so that it fits perfectly within
the natural nooks and corners created by the smooth branches and the wide trunk.
Furthermore, the platforms are not nailed into place but rather supported by the
skilful lashing with bright green cord of the grey planks around the boughs
which support them. This flet appears sturdy but happily weathered; it is
notable, then, that the cords wrapped around the branches have not dug into them
with the passing years. The elves which inhabit these lofty homes must unwrap
the cord frequently and re-lash the platforms so as to avoid harming the great
tree.
name: Flet within the Golden Treetop

desc:
The ground slopes downwards a little from north to west and the moisture visibly
increases. The floor sinks down with even the least amount of weight applied to
it. A thin layer of water coats the ground at all times, making this place a
haven for water-loving creatures.
name: A Dark, Moist Passage

desc:
The soil leading into this corner passage is less damp than the rest of the
room. It seems that the water is being siphoned elsewhere; the dripping of water
in another part of the passage confirms this. The air is dank and musty. The
ceiling shows several straggling roots, hardly able to grab hold of the earth.
name: Dry Corner

desc:
Walls made of a tangle of tree-roots and the dirt spanning the small gaps
between them form this underground room not far below the surface. Enough air
reaches this place to allow the moulds and fungi to thrive here. The earthen
floor is damp and shifts slightly underfoot; any impressions in the floor
quickly fill with muddy water.
name: Natural Cellar

desc:
This underground cavern seems to be made from a complex root system of the trees
above. The roots are so dense that they hold back the dirt, creating a system of
spaces almost like a tunnel. This would-be passageway extends into the darkness
to the south, while one can reach the surface above through a makeshift doorway
below a tree stump.
name: Cavernous Roots

desc:
As you need to cling to the rocks to stop yourself from falling, you are not
able to see much of the surrounding land without considerable effort.
Fortunately the cracks by your feet are large enough to allow you to catch your
breath. The additional height that you have gained with this precarious climb
does not seem worth the effort.
name: The Top of the Crag

desc:
On the outside the wood of this tree is still green, indicating that it has
fallen recently. However, the inside of the tree is brown and dry, as if it has
been hollow for some time; this fact might have contributed to its fall in high
winds. The space inside forms a low tunnel, in which a man would just be able to
crouch. A number of objects are visible in the gloom, the most obvious ones
being a makeshift bed, small stool and table made from tree stumps. A few small
shelves are cut into the walls. These contain a few small cups and some carved
ornaments in the shape of woodland animals.
name: Inside the Hollow Oak

desc:
This small cavern is sheltered by a number of large rocks that protect it from
the elements. A rotten stench and the sound of dripping water permeate the air.
On the ground there is a pool of water, some old bones and many humanoid
footprints. A few bundles are arranged in an orderly row on a natural shelf in
the back of this small cave; the bundles are wrapped in mouldy canvas.
name: Smelly Cave

desc:
The branches of the two great trees merge into one crown. Dry and partly
charred, these branches still appear to be quite strong; stronger perhaps than
they were when the trees were alive and green, as if in death they had obtained
some new strength previously unfamiliar to them. The trunks of the trees are as
hard as if they were petrified, but the branches haven't hardened completely and
still bend slightly under their own weight.
name: Lookout

desc:
The riverbank slopes steeply away from the Anduin on either side, culminating in
the west in a tall, golden-crowned forest. The trunks of these magnificent trees
are smooth and thick; it seems unlikely that any mortal force could sway them.
In the east, a dense, green forest follows the path of a northerly ridge. These
waters are deep and swift as they flow southwards, away from the great river
nexus lying northwards.
name: The Mighty River

desc:
The mighty Anduin undergoes a transition as it curves broadly from north to
east. In the north, the river is wave-filled and swift as it flows away from a
junction of two rivers. Powerful currents work in many directions, all the while
maintaining a downstream flow. Above the eastern riverbank, a high wooded ridge
follows the river on its north-south course. Conversely, as the Anduin moves
east and then south, it widens and becomes less turbulent.
name: Curve in the Anduin

desc:
The banks of the Anduin are widely spaced, yet the river flows swiftly and with
significant force. Several large grey boulders can be seen in shallower water
near the western shore, but they are submerged and create little disturbance in
the smooth river surface. The river flows in from the west, where it bends, and
away to the south. A high, forested ridge rises to the north, echoing the
Anduin's sounds back this way.
name: Swiftly Running River

desc:
These clear waters choose a deep and narrow path as they flow swiftly past
riverbanks carpeted in violet, bell-shaped flowers. In the east, the Celebrant
bravely encounters the mighty Anduin, whose powerful currents shift the course
of both rivers to the south. In the north, a broad golden lawn rises above the
river nexus, bordered to the north by a tall, green wall. The same kind of green
wall can be seen upon the steep southern riverbank.
name: Rapidly Flowing Celebrant

desc:
Chaos ensues as the Anduin and Celebrant crash into each other, their
white-capped currents pounding forcefully against the steep, rocky riverbanks.
Despite the swift fury of the eastern-flowing Celebrant, the Anduin wins the
battle of rivers, incorporating the foreign waters into its southerly course.
The wooded ridge visible to the east becomes a steep cliff as it extends
northwards along Anduin's path. In the northwest, a shining lawn stretches
between the waterways and a thick, green hedge. Beyond this natural wall, the
ground rises and mallorn-trees reach their graceful crowns high above the
horizon.
name: Meeting of the Anduin and Silverlode

desc:
Vast, craggy fissures in the rocky base of the Anduin, combined with the river's
swift current, have caused the current here to be swift and dangerous. Foamy,
white waves smash against the solid stone of the eastern riverbank, which rises
into a tall, wooded cliff. In the west, the terrain rises steeply above water
level, giving way to a glistening lawn of soft golden grasses. Farther in that
direction, golden-crowned mellyrn rise high into the heavens.
name: Tumultuous Anduin

desc:
The Anduin flows swiftly between steep, rocky banks, crested by the golden
crowns of the mallorn-trees. Any attempt to cross from shore to river would be
futile, marred by the overgrown riverbanks and unforgiving current. To the
southeast, the forested ridge bordering the waterway grows in height, forming a
thickly wooded cliff. Beyond the western riverbank lies a break in the lofty
tree-tops, as if there was a clearing in that direction.
name: Amid the Mighty Current

desc:
The crystal-clear waters of the Celebrant flow deeply and quickly beneath tall
woods of silver and gold. The river bends from the north away to the east;
visible on the far side of this bend is a lush lawn of golden shade. To the west
and south, the ancient forests of Lothlorien continue upon rolling, grass and
flower-covered slopes.
name: Bend in the Celebrant

desc:
The Celebrant bends from west to south as its glittering waters wind through the
tall mallorn forests of Lorien. In the north, a faint path rises above the river
level and quickly disappears into the gold-topped forests lying in that
direction. Directly to the east, a white stone hythe has been constructed at the
water's edge; moored to it are several narrow wooden boats, clearly of fine
craftsmanship. Beyond the structure, a broad, shimmering lawn stretches north
and east before encountering a high wall of lush, green bushes.
name: Southward Bend in the River

desc:
Deep and wide, the Celebrant flows steadily eastwards, flanked by the eternally
watching mellyrn that line the riverbank, like sentinels standing the honour
guard. The cold and clear water fills the air with a happy babbling that echoes
into the dense wood on either side, blending with the soft whispers of the
slowly moving tree-branches.
name: Celebrant

desc:
A combination of the rivers to the east, south, and west and the high, green
wall to the north gives this long, shining lawn a sheltered and secretive feel.
The grasses are tall, soft, and pale golden in hue; flowers of sun-golden and
star-silver shade are supported above this living carpet on slender, green
stems. Across the Anduin in the east, mighty currents pound relentlessly against
a sheer stone cliff topped with tall hardwood trees. In the west, a white
structure of stone and wood has been built at the calmer Celebrant's edge.
name: Shining Lawn

desc:
The sound of swiftly flowing water dominates your senses. To the east, a high
wall of soft green turf gives way to the mighty Anduin, which presses powerfully
against the steeply sloped banks as it races southward. Any attempt to ford the
river in that direction would surely result in death. From the north and
southwest come the playful, airy sounds of smaller waterways. Golden-topped
mellyrn rise from the forest floor in all directions but east, like ancient
sentries guarding a sacred clearing. Above, the sky rises high. At your feet,
myriad delicate flowers spring from a lush organic carpet.
name: Opening in the Trees

desc:
Although these woodlands are bordered on all sides by more trees, the area is
distinctly transitional. The forest here and to the north is light and airy,
inhabited predominantly by slender birch trees. A similar environment exists in
the west. Eastwards, a tall, scraggly bunch of trees juts up into the sky and
out against a backdrop of flowing plains. Southwards, the personality of the
forest undergoes a great change, as needly pines and dark earth replace the
deciduous trees and soft turf found here.
name: Under a Canopy of Boughs

desc:
These woodlands are composed almost entirely of slender birch, which rise to a
height of no more than ten yards. The trees grow in clusters between which may
be found stretches of open turf, lending an open, airy feel to the forest.
Grassy meadows lie past the northern and eastern tree line, while to the south
the woodlands continue, with beech and maple trees intermingling with the
familiar birch.
name: Sparse and Open Birches

desc:
The trees in this light and airy forest seem to belong to one of two groups,
groves of which coexist while retaining distinct borders. In some areas clusters
of short birch trees rise from a soft, grassy floor, their pale bark providing a
stark contrast to the deep colours of the surrounding foliage. In other places,
however, the green turf gives way to a brown, earthy, acorn-littered floor from
which sprout groves of tall oak. The forest continues in all directions,
becoming much more dense in the west.
name: Light and Airy Forest

desc:
The trees here are tall, thick and healthy, growing close together and sending
long, arched boughs into every inch of space exposed to the sky. Visibility in
any direction is significantly impeded by the woodland growth, but it appears
that the forest becomes lighter to the north and east. Drifting in from the
southwest are the pounding, splashing, and gurgling sounds of a swiftly flowing
river. The elevation rises in that direction.
name: Deep in the Wooded Vale

desc:
This forested spur of land is made a peninsula by the swiftly flowing river that
wraps around it from west to south. The ground is very soft, indicating that
there may be hollows beneath the grassy turf. Straight beech and paper-skinned
birch rise from the brown soil and lean out over the water, which is deep and
clear. The land rises steeply to the north and slopes away to the east; in both
directions the plant growth is thick and verdant. Several large, grey boulders
rest submerged in the somewhat shallower waters on the far western shore of the
river.
name: Riverside Woods

desc:
This forested ridge reaches a densely wooded, rocky pinnacle before descending
steeply to the swift river lying westward. The high ground continues to the
north and south, whereas the terrain to the east becomes lower and the
vegetation more conservatively spaced. The river beyond the western bank is
noisy and frothing, becoming smooth-surfaced and serene as it bends around to
the south.
name: High Riverbank

desc:
Moving from east to west, the upward slope of this hillside increases as the
vegetation hugging the ground becomes more dense and tangled. Farther to the
east, the low-lying bushes that make this terrain difficult clear out, giving
way to an airy forest of tall-trunked hardwoods and slender birches. A ridge of
thick vegetation continues from the northwest to the south. Distantly but
clearly, the sound of a powerful river can be heard over the western ridge.
name: Forested Incline

desc:
The massive oak trees are by far the dominant feature here, their thick, knobby
trunks a silent testament to the vast age of this forest. Clusters of thin birch
trees sprout in the better-lit areas beneath the natural ceiling of broad oak
branches, their papery bark and light green leaves frail and delicate in
comparison to the ancient giants. The ground slopes upwards to the west, where a
forested ridge can be seen in the distance running on a north-south axis.
name: Amid the Oak and Birch

desc:
An ancient and healthy forest begins here and spreads away to the south and
west. The oak and birch trees that dominate the woodland setting are tall and
well-spaced, so that visibility in those directions is substantial. In the east,
a sheltered meadow is like a hidden cove branching off of the massive sea of
grass which stretches into the northern horizon.
name: Bounds of the Woodland

desc:
Bordered on the south and west by a light forest and to the east by a
brush-covered slope, this green meadow is well sheltered from wind and view. The
grass covering the earth is soft and delicate-bladed, unlike the tall, tough
variety growing in the grasslands visible northwards. Here and there oak and
birch trees sprout from the living carpet, spreading their boughs in a high
ceiling that adds to the hidden feel of this glade.
name: Meadow under the Forest

desc:
Lush, green grasses of several varieties coat rolling slopes like a highly
textured layer of paint. The grasslands continue to the north and east, where
the terrain becomes flatter and windblown and the vegetation is less verdant.
Moving west or south, peaceful meadows give way to the trunks and boughs of a
light, healthy forest.
name: Green and Verdant Grassland

desc:
The plant life inhabiting these plains is more varied here than to the north and
east, where the grasslands roll redundantly off into the horizon. Small flowers
sit atop green shoots which are thin but surprisingly hearty, providing
colourful contrast to the broad-bladed grasses which dominate the landscape. The
ground has a slight upward slope to the south and west, as if it were rising to
meet the hardwood forest which grows there.
name: Plains Nearing the Forest

desc:
The tall grasslands growing unfettered in the north and east have become docile
and unobtrusive as they transition into stately woodlands. In contrast to those
rolling plains, the ground here is covered by a soft, green layer of turf no
more than two inches in height. Thick-trunked oak trees climb high before
spreading their boughs, so that this meadow has a sheltered feel even in broad
daylight. More forest lies south and west.
name: Eaves of the Forest

desc:
Tall hardwoods of myriad varieties cohabitate to form this forest, which
stretches away in all directions. The air carries with it the fresh aroma of
soil rich in organic content. Spotted with occasional tufts of flowers or grass,
the earth is indeed fertile, as indicated by its moist, coarse feel and dark
colour.
name: Green and Healthy Woodland

desc:
Woodlands from the north, south, and east come together at this lofty point to
witness the marriage of two great waterways beyond the western riverbank.
Celebrant, the smaller of the two rivers, splashes energetically as the currents
pull its clear waters into the pounding, irresistible force of the mighty
Anduin. Many miles to the west, beyond this aquatic nexus, the Celebrant can be
seen winding upwards into a tall forest of golden hue.
name: Forest over the River

desc:
This wild outcropping lies on a higher elevation than the surrounding lands,
affording an excellent panoramic view when weather allows. The immediate area is
covered by low, wide trees sprouting from rocky soil between massive, grey
boulders. The earth drops off steeply to the west, where a mighty river pounds
powerfully against a sheer cliff side. Travel in that direction is clearly
impossible. The forested ridge continues to the north and south, while in the
east a thickly forested slope gives way to a healthy forest. Farther off in the
east, the woodlands falter and give way to a wide expanse of plains. Westwards,
across the powerful Anduin, a majestic lawn opens between tall trees of golden
hue. Barely visible beyond this ancient forest, the sharp peaks of a vast
mountain range provide a snow-capped backdrop.
name: Thickly Wooded Cliff

desc:
This forest is dense and overgrown, yet no plant seems to thrive at another's
expense. Visibility in any direction is limited by a layer of smaller trees and
bushes that grow to a height of several yards. Nevertheless, the woodlands to
the east and north seem slightly more airy and open. To the west, the ground
rises steeply upwards to a thickly wooded and rocky pinnacle of land, beyond
which can be heard the sound of rushing water.
name: Verdant Forest

desc:
The soil must be rich and fertile in order to support the lush habitat that
exists here. Small plants and flowers mingle with taller grasses, which in turn
lap at the mighty trunks of great oak and beech trees. Sounds carried down on
the breeze indicate that the forest canopy is rich with birds and other
warm-blooded wildlife. Plains extend to the east, while in the west the
woodlands become too thick for mortal eyes to significantly penetrate.
name: Edge of the Woodlands

desc:
The ground here is relatively flat and covered liberally with thick, hearty
grasses of several varieties. To the south and west, a thin forest can be seen
rising from the sprawling green expanse. In the east, the plains flow into the
horizon, changing in colour from verdant green to weathered yellow.
name: Sprawling Grasslands

desc:
The landscape here is boring and redundant. Plains thickly overgrown with grass
spread out in all directions, unhindered by mountain, forest or stream. The soil
here is hard and the vegetation leathery and yellowish in colour. On the
contrary, the grasslands to the south seem to impart a greener hue on the
horizon. Several miles to the southwest, a change in terrain can barely be
detected.
name: Open Plains

desc:
The grass covering these plains is thick and sharp-edged. The vegetation rises
to an average height of approximately two feet and ranges in spectrum of colour
between yellow and green. Several miles to the west, a thin forest can be seen
rising from the redundant grasslands. Eastward, the vast plains can be seen as
far as visibility allows.
name: Grass-covered Flatland

desc:
The soil here is firm but soft, providing a forgiving surface for the
weary-footed. Verdant grasses grow abundantly from the fertile earth, reaching
over a yard in height. Moving westward, the vegetation becomes softer and taller
still, so that one might initially mistake the line of trees in that direction
for towering plant life of a grassy variety.
name: Tall Grasslands

desc:
A thick forest covers this terrain, which rises steeply to the west before
giving way to a sharp, brush-covered drop off. Beyond this natural boundary, a
tumultuous river slides past banks of moss-covered stone and black soil. On the
western bank, trees of magnificent stature grow high towards the heaven,
culminating in graceful crowns of golden hue. The ground slopes downwards to the
east, yielding more forest before plains eventually dominate the landscape
several miles in the distance. To the south the ground level rises slightly,
giving way to a rugged peak which might allow a decent view of the surroundings.
name: Steep Forest

desc:
The beech and oak trees that form this forest are narrow-trunked and evenly
spaced, so that the floor beneath their boughs is well lit and airy. The area
between the trees is covered by a spongy, green, moss-like plant, that quickly
springs back to its original height after being trodden upon. Several clusters
of small flowers grow in the shadows of some of the smaller trees, their violet
blossoms resembling five-pointed stars. The ground rises sharply to the west,
where the forest becomes dense and wild.
name: Light Woodland

desc:
This is a land of dichotomy. A healthy forest extends far to the west, while
vast grass-covered plains dominate the eastern landscape. Here, the fingers of
each habitat clasp together to form a land of transition, where woodlands and
grasslands coexist. Tall, hearty shoots of grass lap at the smooth-barked trunks
which rise straight out of the earth, culminating in crowns of curved boughs and
symmetrical leaves.
name: Forest Border

desc:
The forest here is tall and imposing, thick with growth in shades of green and
gold. Upon initial inspection, it would seem to take hours to move even a short
distance through this verdant woodland. A closer look at the soft carpet of
grass and flowers reveals a faint pathway meandering northwards. Whoever built
this trail seemed to do so with the intent of harming as little wildlife as
possible. The gurgle and splash of a swift stream reaches your ears from beyond
the trees to the south. To the east, the forest thins out, and a sweet-smelling
breeze drifts in from that direction.
name: A Faint Path

desc:
The bank of the Celebrant slopes steeply southward, almost as if the forest
itself longed to join the clear water as it rushes away eastwards toward the
great river Anduin. Yet the trees stand dense upon the bank, giving little heed
to the rocky slope that lies beneath them and about their great grey roots.
name: Bank of Celebrant

desc:
Nestled within a patch of tall grass and reeds, this structure is designed to
house a variety of slender boats and barges. The floor is constructed of long
wooden planks, laid side by side and painted white, extending about a yard out
over the surface of the water. Several niches of open water protrude into the
boathouse, alongside of which sturdy metal rings have been bolted into the floor
beams for the purpose of securing the boats. On each corner of the hythe jutting
into the river a stone pylon anchored beneath the clear waters supports the
floor. Walls of meticulously interlocked stones rise from the planks on the
north and eastern sides of the structure; like the planks below, these smooth
stones have been painted a clean shade of white. A simple white door made from
three planks fitted together lies in the eastern wall. Hooks secured into the
walls support oars, rope, and other nautical tools. Above, a vaulted wooden
ceiling protects inhabitants from the elements.
name: Hythe on the Celebrant

desc:
A massive, weathered boulder sits atop this forested ridge, almost entirely
covering a shallow crevice which dips into the rocky ground. Several
wide-trunked hardwoods provide cover from the elements on either side of the
boulder, so that this natural shelter is well-hidden from the outside. The floor
is damp and soft, covered with a thick layer of decaying foliage.
name: Sheltered Hollow

desc:
Small dew-drops glisten gently upon the lush undergrowth, set here by the foamy
fingers of the southward lying river. The mallorn-trees are tall and strong, as
daunting as mountains yet somehow as delicate as newly-sprouted blades of grass.
The woodlands continue to the north, west, and east.
name: Golden Wooded Slope

desc:
Golden-crested trees upon high banks rise proudly on either side of this river
as it meanders steadily over rounded grey stones speckled with black. The water
appears clear and fresh. To the east the river begins to alter its course from
east-west to north-south.
name: Celebrant

desc:
The air is moist here and filled with the sounds of water flowing gently among
the great trunks of the mellyrn. The joyous sound of the rushing Celebrant
blends with the song of hidden birds to create a serene background of peace and
tranquility.
name: In the Vivid Forest

desc:
The calm waters of the Celebrant form a smooth bend to the north and east,
creating a short, rounded peninsula of tree-topped land. Proud mellyrn of
unfathomable age are rooted firmly in the soil, surrounded by myriad species of
lush, green grass. Here and there long-stemmed flowers sprout from the soft
floor, crowned with diamond-shaped petals of pale green and blue.
name: Amid the Lush Mallorn Forest

desc:
The mallorn-trees are packed closely together, so that looking up you only see a
golden sky of intertwined tree-limbs punctuated by flashes of brilliant silver.
In every direction the forest radiates a thousand hues of green and gold.
Shadows abound in the west, but no sense of evil radiates from that direction.
The breeze carries with it sad melodies of songs unknown or long forgotten.
name: Deep in the Golden Forest

desc:
The shadows lie deep amongst the grey trunks of the mellyrn as they stand tall
and dense here, dispelling all light before it reaches the forest floor.
Nevertheless, the shadows are not hostile as they are a natural part of the
woods. You can barely discern shapes moving within them, happily accepting the
shade for what it is. Despite the lack of direct sunlight, the undergrowth has
grown here thick enough to hinder movement.
name: Shadowed Woods

desc:
Standing tall and proud, the grey and golden mellyrn rise high towards the sky,
almost as if reaching for the beloved stars of the Galadhrim. The forest is
quiet and solemn, disturbed only by the birds singing softly from the cover of
the lush green boughs and the soft whispering of the wind as it gently rocks the
swaying trees.
name: Among the Mellyrn

desc:
You find yourself at the very edge of the Golden Wood. North and west, the grey
and golden mellyrn of Lothlorien rise towards the sky as a reminder of the
mystery and power residing within. To the east, the forest is of another kind,
still ancient and alive, but not of the Golden Wood. Southward the terrain opens
up into a grassy plain.
name: Before the Forest

desc:
Beech, oak, and poplar trees grow tall and straight in this forest, many winters
having passed since their conception. Nevertheless, the most ancient tree in
this woodland appears a mere sapling when compared with the great mallorn-trees
that tower above you in the north. The silence here is thick and pervasive, yet
these woods do not feel unwatched.
name: Deep Forest

desc:
The tall, straight trunks of the mallorn trees are spaced so that each has
precisely enough room to spread its golden crown high in the sky. The limbs of
these ancient giants are broad and strong, growing off the main trunks at wide,
upswept angles. Soft, delicate sounds, like a combination of song and laughter,
drift down from the natural ceiling, but the foliage is too dense to discover
their origin. The forest floor is soft and grassy; here and there white and pale
green flowers with star-shaped petals poke up through the green carpet. In other
places, larger, star-shaped blossoms of silver and gold sit upon slender, green
stalks.
name: Mallorn Forest

desc:
The forest is ancient and magical, powerful and fragile at the same time.
Smooth, silvery trunks give way to a natural ceiling of gold and grey, suspended
upon upswept limbs of considerable girth. In contrast to these sturdy giants,
who seem an extension of the very rock of the earth, tiny bell-shaped flowers of
myriad pastel colours dance amid a floor of soft, thin-bladed grass. To the
north, a lively river splashes noisily between rocky banks, sheltered overhead
by the overhanging mallorn limbs.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
The vegetation here is thick at all levels of the forest. Near ground level
tangled bushes give way to closely packed tree trunks which in turn blossom into
a ceiling of intertwined limbs and leaves. Thick woodlands continue to the east
and west, while in the south a wide trail begins and winds away into the
undergrowth. Most stunning of the surrounding features are the tall,
glittery-crowned mallorn-trees which grow in the north, marking the borders of
ancient elven Lothlorien. A strange, eerie aura encompasses the golden forest,
which seems to shift slightly each time a traveller looks away and then back at
it.
name: Dense and Intertwined Woodland

desc:
This is a definite and much used trail skirting the forest which stands to your
east and north. It peters out to the north with many fine trails leading off
into the trees in the north and east. Many feet have worn away the grasses a
stones throw from the trees. The trail runs south where grass covers the rolling
flatlands. The rough lands to the west are sparsely covered and exposed to the
elements.
name: Worn Trail

desc:
Large stones on the trail make progress a little uncomfortable. Tree-roots seem
to have broken up the flagstones that used to pave this way. The trail runs
north-south with fields to the west and a forest to the east. Light winds make a
murmuring sound in the leaves of the trees. The great forest covers the horizon
to the north and east.
name: Worn Trail

desc:
Near the corner of the forest, between the stony trail and the trees, there is a
clear circle of worn grass, indicative of a large number of travellers and their
mounts that once camped here. The circle is ringed with a dozen blackened fire
circles. The edge of the forest is nearest to the northeast and runs in a dark
line southeast. A well-defined trail runs north-south. Scant grasslands lie to
the west.
name: Abandoned Camp

desc:
Although this was once a well-kept trail, weeds and grasses cover most of the
broken flagstones showing that it is now seldom used. The trail runs north-south
with grasslands to the west and a forest some distance away to the east. The
lands to your south are increasingly wilder and more desolate. The dark grey of
the horizon to the north and west shows the vast extent of the forest.
name: Trail

desc:
The paving stones that once covered this part of the trail have been broken or
looted, leaving large potholes. Many of these are now filled with water, making
it harder to follow the trail. A few tracks at the side of the trail show that
this way still sees some light use. The trail continues north and south. The
shadow of a forest rises to the east with bare grasslands to the west. A
stagnant smell fills the air.
name: Trail

desc:
Clearly marked by a strip of bright green grass, the trail passes through a
small depression. The ground is soft and wet. In places you see small animal
tracks in the tall grass leading off to either side of the trail. A grey
leafless forest stands a short distance to the east. The air is still, humid and
smells of damp grass.
name: Grassy Trail

desc:
This stretch of grass to the west of the large forest is pocked with molehills
and rabbit holes. Near the forest edge the activity of these small animals has
exposed the roots of the trees. The soil is iron-brown and gradually darkens to
the south. A north-south trail is clearly visible just to the west. The odd
noise of a small bird or animal can be heard near the tree line.
name: Rough Field

desc:
A few broken flagstones have been piled up here to stand a few inches higher
than the long grasses which are the dominant vegetation in this terrain. A few
rough marks have been scratched into the top stone, but they have been weathered
to such an extent that they have no meaning to you. A trail lies about twenty
paces to your west and the dark outline of a forest is visible to your east.
name: Small Cairn by the Trail

desc:
The ground here is untrodden. Several different types of long grasses are mixed
together, with the odd small flower struggling to survive in the poor soil. The
forest stands silently to the east like a shadow on a winter afternoon. To the
west you can make out a trail running north-south.
name: Rough Grasses

desc:
The edge of the forest can be seen to the west and south. Here the trees stand
quite closely, yet the woods remain light and airy. Animals and birds can be
heard rustling in the underbrush, darting among the branches and calling to each
other, perhaps alarmed by your presence. A light, warm breeze moves the branches
of the trees.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
Green mosses and lichens cover the trunks of the living trees in this part of
the forest. Springing from under the tree-roots, a small stream tumbles down a
tree-lined crag. The small waterfall creates a constant spray, keeping the air
damp. The rivulet meanders eastwards where it descends a low slope. Little other
sounds can be heard over the noise of the water.
name: Green Forest

desc:
This is the very edge of the woodland. The forest here is green and cool. A
light breeze rustles in the leaves above, where small birds sing their
springtime songs. To the north and east the trees get thicker. To the west and
south the forest ends giving way to grasslands. A variety of small trails and
animal tracks disappear into the trees but end as abruptly as they start.
name: Woodland

desc:
The trees stand shoulder to shoulder like guards; perhaps they will not let
anything evil pass. There is a distinct feel of springtime here. New growth and
bright green leaves on the trees are testament to that. You feel a light breeze
from the grasslands to the west. In the distance you hear what could be laughter
or perhaps just the trickling of water.
name: Thick Woods

desc:
A clear circle of worn grass lies between you and a trail to the west. Next to
you a small circle of ashes ringed with stones the size of a man's hand marks
the edge of the camp. Although the camp appears long deserted, a few broken
pots, camp wear and animal droppings give you some clue of the nature of those
who passed. The forest stands proudly to the north and east. The whispering of
the leaves is reassuring in this otherwise silent place.
name: At the Edge of a Camp

desc:
The bushes in the plains to the west give way to poorly covered grassland. The
soil is sandy, rocky in places, and unable to sustain more than a few hardy
grasses. You are very exposed here and would perhaps be safer on the edge of the
forest that lies close to the north and continues on to the east and west. The
air is dry, relatively warm and carries the smell of the woodlands.
name: Rough Plains

desc:
Flat and quite featureless, the grass-carpeted plains continue. There seems to
be little evidence of any other inhabitants; the surrounding ground bears no
signs of any permanent dwellings of the local wildlife. The edge of the Golden
Wood rises to the north, its heady scent already tingling the air here.
name: Level Grasslands

desc:
The grasslands begin to thin out slightly here, leading into more barren areas
to the east. The land is flat in all directions, and mostly featureless save for
a few patches of stunted grass and bushes that are more bramble than leaf. The
wind carries a hint of the Golden Wood as it wafts lazily from the northwest.
name: Level Grasslands

desc:
Rocks, stones, poor soil, eroding winds and lack of water have combined to make
these lands bare and devoid of vegetation save a few tufts of hardy grass. When
the wind strengthens it picks up grit and sand making travel uncomfortable. To
the east you can see a well defined trail running along the edge of a large
forest. The landscape in other directions is unremarkable with only an odd tree,
outcrop of rocks or bush to break up its monotony.
name: Sparse Plains

desc:
Thorny bushes and thistles cover much of the flat ground here. The brambles
snatch at your clothes and skin. These plants provide cover for a variety of
small animals and shelter you from any would be pursuers. There are some dark
berries on the brambles.
name: Scrubland

desc:
These plains consist mostly of low, light shrubbery growing in clumps among the
coarse grass. The soil is barren and not very fertile, yet some bushes seem to
thrive here. Occasionally something can be seen rustling in the bushes farther
away; perhaps a rabbit or another small animal. The scrubland continues
eastwards.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
Bushes dominate this mostly barren landscape. Light shrubbery grows everywhere,
but does not make travelling difficult. The bushes are of a low and tangled kind
and seem to tolerate the dry climate fairly well. Some tough grasses seem to
thrive in more densely populated areas of brush. The scrubland continues to the
north and east, and an old path lies to the south.
name: Bushes by the Path

desc:
The old path, faint but still discernible, runs east-west through the
wilderness. Here it is now little more than an animal track, and is probably
very seldom trodden, yet it appears that it once served many a traveller. Plains
lie in all directions, and to the north, the Golden Wood can be spied in the
distance.
name: An Old Path

desc:
The bushes and brambles to the north gradually peter out as the soil gets poorer
and dryer. Small clumps of yellowing grass cling together, barely surviving the
wind due to their long roots. There are many large patches of bare earth and
stones where nothing grows. A few burrows show that some small animals manage to
live here. To the east you pick out a trail in the plains, and far beyond it
lies the shadow of a large forest.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
The ground in these bare lands seems to suffer from extremes of weather and
bears little or no vegetation. The soil is baked hard with gaping cracks which
would be an ideal refuge for small animals or insects. The bleached skeleton of
a large animal, perhaps a horse, lies here. The lands to the north and west are
unremarkable. The only feature to the east is a green trail cutting through the
dryer grasses that cover the surrounding plains. The fields to the south show
signs of habitation.
name: Cracked Earth

desc:
Rough, rocky debris is now the only thing present on this bleak valley edge
which once used to be fertile, arable land, most likely the property of some
farm. This slope has been left without tending for many years, and its bare
brown and yellow soil allows little vegetation to grow.
name: Ruined Fields

desc:
This part of the forest is populated by tall pine trees. There is little
vegetation at ground level, but areas of rocky outcrops prevent easy travel in
any direction, often making it necessary for a traveller to retrace his trail to
make progress. At high points one can see a barren plain stretch out to the
east. The forest continues to the west where it is perched on the cliffs. To
your southwest and below you can hear the distant roar of river rapids.
name: Forest between River and Plain

desc:
Here a wood of mixed trees clings to the eastern bank of the river Anduin. Here
and there you can see some structures high in the trees. Judging by the manner
of their construction they might well be elven, although there is no sight or
sound of those people. The forest continues in all directions except to the
west, although to the east the trees seem to thin out.
name: Bright Woods

desc:
The trees in these woods by the river stand close together. Their thick foliage
