prevents you from seeing far in any direction, but you can hear the river rapids
some distance to the southwest. The ground rises steeply to the south and
gradually to the east. Some of the trees seem to be in blossom. The ground is
covered with fragrant herbs.
name: Thick Woods by the River

desc:
Rising atop the sheer cliff that forms the east bank of the great river, a line
of grey, sparsely clad trees marks the river's edge. The noise of the river
tumbling over rapids comes from the west. The sound fades to the south as the
river's flow becomes calmer after the turn. To the east the forest stretches out
for some distance, thick with undergrowth.
name: Trees upon the Riverbank

desc:
The tree trunks in the forest are large in girth and towering out of the ground
closely together. Along the base of the trees the undergrowth is thick and very
tangled. A few large vines have climbed the old trees so far above, that they
seem to disappear into the leafy branches. The leaves of the trees are dark
green, and the bark protecting each trunk mainly dark grey or deep brown.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
The trees here are tall and tightly-packed, in sharp contrast to the well-spaced
woodlands which extend westwards. Grassy fields lie to the north and east,
sculpting from the vast forests a wooded peninsula jutting out into a sea of
rolling plains.
name: A Spur of Forest

desc:
A flat, grassy meadow is here, bordered to the south and west by woodlands and
to the north by a stretch of brush-filled plains. Small flowers dot the lawn,
their triangle-shaped petals discarded seasonally and blown about by the winds.
Farther to the west, the ground rises into a densely forested ridge of land
which can be seen extending on a northerly plain.
name: Meadow Bordering the Woodland

desc:
In contrast to the seemingly never-ending grasslands that extend away to the
north and west, this sloping terrain is covered by patches of dense brush. Small
red berries growing in clusters give a polka-dot appearance to these bushes,
which rise to a height of three to five feet. In the west and south, a serene
meadow rests under the boughs of tall oak-trees.
name: Red-berried Bushes

desc:
These plains are generally flat and covered by a thick layer of grass which has
been matted down by countless years of sporadic windstorms. The grass blades are
tangled and intertwined, creating difficult footing for land-roving creatures.
Short brush grows to the south, beyond which a forest can be seen rising from
the horizon. To the west, the grass appears less trampled.
name: Windblown Plains

desc:
Travellers on foot must wade through tall, thick grass in order to traverse
these plains. The footing is somewhat tricky, as downward visibility is poor and
exposed roots form lumpy knots of dirt and vegetation. The terrain looks similar
in all directions, if slightly more hilly to the north.
name: Grass-covered Plains

desc:
Grassy plains stretch away for miles in every direction, swaying rhythmically
with the movement of air across these open lands. The soil beneath this sea of
plant life is coarse and rocky, which probably accounts for the struggling,
faded appearance of the vegetation.
name: Open Grassland

desc:
All sorts of grass cover the plain here and form much more than only one meadow.
At some spots, the grass is thin, yet high, while elsewhere, it grows low with
broad leaves and a thick, healthy green colour. Where two or more of these
meadows meet, they mix and form an area which seems nearly colourful, yet it
only contains many shades of green. Only very low bushes peek out of the sea of
grass, and some of them are nearly overgrown by patches of particularly high
grass.
name: Many Meadows in the Plains

desc:
Swaying and bowing in the rhythm of occasional breezes, a large, obviously wild
wheat field fills the plains, surrounded by a large green meadow, stretching
only to the south. A few rowans and cherry trees grow in it, offering shadow
during hot summer days. A few tiny furrows running through the ground create
waves in the wheat, making it look like a yellow sea.
name: Wheat Field

desc:
Northwards, a solid looking wall of briars and pines slowly and smoothly blends
into a field of golden wheat that is enclosed by green grass to the west. Dog
roses arise amidst the wheat, growing in a few spread clusters, while bluebells
and coltsfoot can be seen in abundance among it.
name: Briars Leading to a Field

desc:
A field of high grass mixed with thorny weeds turns into a tangled wood of
briars more and more to the north, building a living wall of thorns. Small,
crouched pine trees are visible among the briars, making the thicket even harder
to pass. Small scarlet bushes with large clusters of red berries grow in small
patches, adding bright dots in the colour of the landscape.
name: Briars and Bushes

desc:
Manifold flowers grow among high green grass, filling the meadows with different
colours during the course of the seasons, apart from winter. A single, high
willow tree overlooks the field, its branches reaching out wide and low down to
the grass. To the north, the vegetation becomes denser, and thorny shrubs take
over the place of the flowers.
name: Flowery Meadows

desc:
Lush green grass inhabits the fields, crowded with wildflowers of manifold sizes
and colours. The plains expand far into the forests to the south and the west,
creating deep grassy pockets in the woods. Close to the borderline where the
forests and the plains meet, bushes and smaller trees have claimed their share
of the ground, and ferns are rather common in the eastern parts.
name: Plains Meeting the Forest Outskirts

desc:
The transition of the forest, from the meadow to the east, takes place here.
There are numerous saplings which have sprouted from the earth. Each sapling has
a light-coloured brown bark at the base where it meets the ground. Developing
branches on the saplings are still quite green though. The trees along the outer
edge of the forest are small in comparison to the ones that are deeper within.
There are a few pine trees growing within the forest transition, their needles
being soft green in colour. Eastward, there is a meadow filled with different
lengths of grass. A forest cabin stands to the north.
name: Forest Transition

desc:
The walls of the cabin were made from some red stained wood, probably from the
trees of the surrounding forest. Decorations of leaves and drying floral
arrangements adorn the high timber ceiling and sturdy walls. A polished oaken
table stands in the centre of the room, beside an empty bed. The bed has a
wooden frame, ornamented with elegant embellishments. A soft rug, made of animal
fur, lies on the smooth floorboards in front of the fireplace. The cabin seems
uninhabited but frequently visited, perhaps providing shelter during long
hunting trips.
name: Inside a Cabin

desc:
The forest is not heavily wooded here at all. The trees reach to a tall height
above, the sky being visible still due to the spacing between the trees. Each
tree has a slightly different shape to it. Some of the trees are wide, their
branches being very full of leaves. Other trees are more slender in appearance,
as the branches are very thin and do not bear many leaves. The bark that
protects the trunks of these trees displays an assortment of colours. A few of
the trees have bark of a light-coloured grey and leaves in the shape of a
diamond. The leaves are soft green and appear as though they were made of silk.
Other trees in this forest have different tones of brown on their trunks and
leaves of dark green.
name: Light Forest

desc:
This area of the forest becomes closely wooded as it continues to the west, then
begins to slope gently downhill. In the east, the trees begin to thin out as
they meet the edge of a brushwood. To the south the trees are cluttered together
and the undergrowth surrounding them seems almost impassable. There are tiny
vines of ivy attempting to scale the trunks of many of the trees here. In the
centre of every ivy leaf, there is a soft white stripe. There are a few fallen
branches upon the ground, surrounded by a thin bed of grass.
name: In the Forest

desc:
The dense forest surrounds the riverbank here. Massive trees are looming above,
with their uplifted roots making it difficult to travel. Some of these roots are
even cluttering the riverbank as they take a dip into the rushing water of the
Great River. The only passable way from here would be to attempt to cross the
swift waters of the river to the south, or take a chance of surviving the dark
journey under the trees of the forest.
name: Forest along a Riverbank

desc:
The trunks of the trees in the thicket are slender and have a silvery tone to
their bark. A majority of the trees are short in height in comparison to the
trees looming above to the west. Each leaf on the trees is light green on the
top, and underneath they are white. Several blades of grass have grown in the
thicket as well, quite refined and a soft colour of green. On the east side of
the thicket, a large tree has fallen to the ground and is starting to rot.
Looking above the trees of the thicket, a tower of some kind can be seen
standing in the distant south.
name: Dense Thicket

desc:
There are a lot of bushes and shrubs growing in the brushwood. Debris from the
forest lies in pieces of various sizes on the ground. Some small trees are
growing in the thicket to the south of the brushwood. A few brown shrubs with an
abundance of thorns growing from their short branches are spread out among the
brush here. Only a few blades of dried-up grass can be noticed, with the rest of
the ground being composed of dry and bare soil.
name: In the Brushwood

desc:
Many sage plants are growing here in the meadow. The plants have a two-toned
colour of grey and green, blending in well with the light-coloured grey bark of
the forest to the west. Encircling the sage plants in the meadow are blades of
grass that appear closely grown together in bundles. Most of these blades are a
lighter shade of green.
name: Meadow of Sage

desc:
The meadow expands in all directions other than west, where a forest can be
vaguely seen. Grass consumes most of the meadow, along with a few weeds. Most of
the weeds are long, slender and brown in colour, some of them have dried flowers
at their tips. Several tree saplings have sprung up from within the earth as
well. These saplings are extremely young since their bark is still a light shade
of green. The grass displays a different shade of green, along with a few
patches that are golden brown.
name: In the Meadow

desc:
A river coming from the east leads to a waterfall, tumbling down over a couple
of rocky patios resulting in spray and myriads of tiny water drops filling the
air. With a thundering noise, most of it finally disappears somewhere behind the
spumy curtain, while some of the water flows over the patios and forms a tiny
lake below the waterfall. Ferns and moss prosper everywhere near it, while lush
green grass, rarely interrupted by a small sapling, dominates the surrounding
meadows.
name: Waterfall in the Plains

desc:
Perched in a weird formation by forces of nature, large red blocks of sandstone
solemnly rest atop a small pedestal of rock. It is likely that their order is a
result of common winds laden with dust, eroding a tower of sandstone over
centuries. The stones are encircled by thick, green grass, and the air smells
fresh, sometimes carrying a faint scent of water from the south and resin from
the north when appropriate winds carry it along.
name: Strange Rock Formation

desc:
Embedded in a wide valley with overhanging walls, long and high ferns thrive
below large firs and spruces. Encompassed by the ferns, moss nearly completely
disguises rocks, and broad, intertwined briars form a dense, thorny thicket,
forcing even the most skilled rider to lead his mount. A dark, hollow pathway,
shielded by the treetops against the sun, leads through this wilderness.
name: Hollow Forest

desc:
Hissing and tumbling, water rushes along a river, filling the air with its sound
and smell. Lush grass flourishes on its banks, interrupted by hibiscus bushes
and dandelions, and inclined trees hang their branches wide over the stream,
casting shady places on it. The surrounding plains are clad in gold and green,
their colours coming from the high grass populating them.
name: Stream Passing Rocks

desc:
A wide basin with high and overhanging walls lies embedded in the nearby plains,
about a mile in radius. High firs inhabit it, and among them, a subspecies with
hard, spiky and triangular warts instead of needles makes travelling in this
forest painful for a careless wanderer. The air seems somewhat hotter and wetter
than one would guess, further aggravating travel conditions, and often loud,
sharp noises of animals can be heard. A tree much higher than all others grows
close to the northern wall.
name: Forested Valley

desc:
A steep rock spike with a flat plateau, seemingly polished by the forces of
centuries of wind, looms here over the bed of grass surrounding it. Myriads of
dandelions and daisy chains sprout among the high, green grass. Near the base of
the rock spike, a spring gurgles forth from a large crack into a small basin of
granite.
name: At a Cliff

desc:
A few trees have taken root in the plain. High grass grows near their base,
thick tendrils have chosen their trunks as place to grow closer to the light by
climbing up their brown bark. The sounds of things living here can be heard all
around. Unseen insects and vermin create a constant humming and rustling in the
background. To the south, a steep cliff arises from the nearly flat plain and
casts a huge shadow, which only disappears completely in the darkest and
moonless nights. Like a guardian of the northern parts, a huge and ancient row
of bushes blocks sight and way in that direction.
name: Below a Steep Cliff

desc:
Clustered tightly with the rare exception of a few small, narrow pine trees
spotted amidst them, large magnolia bushes fill the plains, drowning them in a
sea of blooms during spring. Bright green grass fills the gaps between and under
the magnolias, and a few clusters of wildflowers protrude at places where the
grass is lower. Many obvious traces of activities of small animals are visible,
and occasionally some of their noises can be heard.
name: Magnolia-filled Plains

desc:
Not long ago, this plain has been a wild orchard of apple trees. Since then,
most of the trees, some of them being quite old, have been felled. A great fire
has burned here, and the gnawed remains of many animals lie around scattered.
Many of the trees seem to have been felled for no reason, since they have been
left here to decay.
name: A Felled Orchard

desc:
Spirals of earth, formed by numerous eddies over many years, interconnect and
weave upon the plains into a strange, twirled pattern. The vegetation, mostly
long, soft, golden grass, has adapted to the shape of the ground below. It shows
the same circular constellations as it, shimmering like glowing runes when
sunlit.
name: Spiral-shaped Dunes

desc:
A small river in the plains coming from the east passes a few narrows on its way
to the south-west. They are created by some boulders in the streambed, making
the river rush rapidly past them, foaming and gurgling. Birches and willows seam
the flanks of the water, highlighting them and dipping their branches deeply in
it, and in spring, their pollen creates yellow cords on the surface of the
current. Beyond the trees, high green and golden grass flourishes, spreading
over the plains and overflowing the ground.
name: Cataracts in a Small Stream

desc:
A small bubbling stream coming from the north slowly changes its course and
turns westward in a long curve, as if an invisible barrier forced it to bend.
The vegetation of the plains, mostly grass, is rich and lively nearby, growing
untamed and high. Animals passing by have left their signs, as here and there a
patch of grass is pressed down. A constant sound like muffled thunder is carried
along when the wind blows from the west.
name: Bend in a Small River

desc:
This area of the meadow is directly north of a wide, grassy plateau. At the base
of the plateau, there are long blades of grass, stretching up high towards the
sky above. There are small patches of weeds mixed among the green grass. Most of
the weeds are prickly, with fine spikes extending from their leaves. The leaves
on the weeds are shaped like spearheads and rather small in size. The meadow
continues to expand to the east.
name: Meadow North of a Plateau

desc:
Atop the plateau, much of the surroundings is visible. A brushwood begins to
fade to the south, where a lot of debris can be noticed on the ground. There is
a meadow to the north of the plateau, filled with long green grass. The footing
here appears to be stable. Being covered by grass, it is easy to notice how the
ground is truly flat on the plateau. The grass is of a soft colour and shorter
in length than the grass that arises around the slopes of the plateau.
name: Grassy Plateau

desc:
The brushwood diminishes from the larger portion of it to the west. Shrubs that
are of different sizes and colours are in growth. Many broken tree branches lie
scattered around on the ground. Some of the branches are skinny, others are
truly bulky. Stuck in some of the brush are dried-up leaves that must have
drifted in from the forest. To the east there is a meadow filled with grass, and
a grassy plateau arises just north of the brushwood.
name: Diminishing Brushwood

desc:
The grass of the meadow is lengthy, each blade being slender and green. To the
south, where the forest stands, the undergrowth is very noticeable. The plants
become tangled right along the eastern edge where the open meadow begins. Many
weeds are growing all over, though some of the grass is much higher, causing a
large majority of the weeds to be overlooked. Together they seem to conceal much
of the slightly rocky ground.
name: Meadow nearing a Forest

desc:
The surface on the top of this cliff is nearly flat and offers a good stand. A
few big rocks and protrusions offer shelter against strong winds, while the view
over the mostly flat landscape around is reached only by few places.
name: On a Flat Plateau

desc:
This section of the forest lies just north of a gigantic pine tree. The pine
tree must obscure most of the sunlight and moisture that would land down here,
for most of the smaller trees under it look slightly withered and worn. The
fields to the east look grassy and should be easy to travel through.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
Light-coloured ferns grow all over the ground here. The ferns have spread out
from the forest that stands to the west, along with many other types of
undergrowth. Most of the ferns are several feet high, their soft green colour
standing out from the rest of the plants that are closer to the edge of the
forest. The forest itself appears to be very dense, dark, and full of growth.
The plains begin to expand towards the east from the bracken field.
name: Bracken Field

desc:
The river is deep and dark here. Popping bubbles along the surface of the water
show that this section of the river is not completely devoid of life. The
constantly moving river turns to the south, while in the east there is a tall
pine tree, a rarity in the soft soil of this area.
name: Deep Bend in the River Anduin

desc:
Lush from the moderate rainfall and temperate climate, the plains here look wide
and inviting. A relatively barren clearing lies eastwards, its red-brown soil a
stark contrast to the bright green here. There are a few small bushes which may
bear fruit when the season is right.
name: Fertile Plains

desc:
A large, rotting pine trunk lies on the ground here, blocking travel to the
west. Judging by the jagged stump nearby, the flexible body of this tall tree
must have snapped from powerful winds. Off to the east, there are several other
trunks that were certainly cut down from the surrounding forest, as these logs
are stripped bare of their branches already. A few stumps still stick out of the
earth here, while only a couple living trees remain standing.
name: Trimmed Forest

desc:
The forest of pines here appears to be thinner than the range of lush and
deciduous forest to the west. Brown pine needles litter the ground, and a few
weeds have sprouted from the earth. To the east, the large conifers give way to
tall grasses and diminutive weeds, signalling the edge of the forest. The
trampled ground and some blackened pieces of wood inside a circle of stones
indicate that this spot has been used as a campsite.
name: Camp along the Forest

desc:
Tall weeds, grasses of every size and tiny flowers dot this section of the
riverbank. The soft mud near the river's edge gives way to much firmer ground
farther away. This has allowed small saplings to take root as an extension of
the forest. To the west lies the winding Anduin River.
name: Plains near the Riverbank

desc:
The riverbank here is covered with thick mud composed of dirt and decaying
materials that are being washed downstream. There are only short grasses and
dirty weeds growing here, but intermittently a fungi-covered tree trunk can be
seen decaying in the muck. The river lies to the west, while to the east and
northeast there appears to be a large field.
name: Muddy Riverbank

desc:
The plants that occupy these plains are gentle and tall. The grasses and weeds
look as though they will rustle and sway at the slightest breeze. A thin layer
of flat grass covers the dirt, probably felled by pouring rain or harsh winds.
name: Gentle Plains

desc:
A small pond is here, in the centre of the meadow, surrounded by long blades of
green grass. There are several types of weeds growing among the grass here as
well. Some are extremely tall and lean, while others are wide and short. The
water of the pond looks very clean and clear, and has several long-stemmed
plants growing along the edges. The spear-shaped leaves upon these plants are of
a dark green colour.
name: Small Pond in the Meadow

desc:
The ground here is covered by grass, but drops to the north rather suddenly.
There is a dale below the shelf to the north, where there probably was once a
small stream flowing through. Several weeds are scattered in bundles here, dark
green in colour and with small leaves upon their stems. The shelf seems to fade
away both to the east and west, but with less grass growing in the eastern
direction.
name: Grassy Shelf

desc:
Grass emerges from the shelf to the west, but is cut off by dried and cracked
ground here. Only a few weeds have survived to keep growing, and only from
within the cracks along the ground. Most of the water from the rain probably
seeks its way into the cracks, giving these weeds enough nutrients to survive.
There are stones of various sizes scattered across the ground, with different
textures and colours. Some smaller pebbles cover the ground in sections to the
east. To the north, there is a steep ledge that leads down to a small dale.
name: Rocky Shelf

desc:
A small ledge begins to form in the centre of these dried grounds. It slowly
gains height as it climbs to the west, with a few rocks mixed into the soil. The
ground is dry and only few blades of grass have survived here, with three small
bushes standing on the eastern side of the ledge. The bushes are short, and
their leaves are crisp and dying.
name: Cracked Ledge

desc:
The ground is rough and cracked, with very small pebbles upon it in random piles
all over. The remains of a streambed can be noticed by the markings and patterns
along the ground, travelling east and west. A slight ledge forms in the south
and appears to grow higher as it continues westwards. The ledge to the north is
very tall, with some green grass noticeable on the top, continuing both east and
west.
name: Dried-up Streambed

desc:
The dale is half covered by small pebbles on the west side, and dry patches of
bare earth on the east. An occasional weed can be noticed, sprouting up from the
earth between the pebbles and looking very dry and unhealthy. To the north and
south are large walls of natural earth. This dale, long ago though, was most
likely a small and quiet stream, but has since dried up and remained bare.
name: Small Dale

desc:
The ground of the dale is covered by rocks and tiny pebbles. Portions of bare
and cracked earth can be noticed, with only a few scrawny weeds trying to grow
there. The dale appears to continue to the north and east, while there are large
walls of natural earth both to the west and to the south. On the ledge above, a
bed of grass is growing. Clumps of dirt fallen from above lie along the base of
the walls.
name: Small Rocky Dale

desc:
On both the east and west sides of the dale are large walls of solid earth. Some
grass can be noticed high above, on top of these walls, but nothing else has
grown out the sides of it other than a few stray roots. The ground is covered
with small pebbles and rocks, mixed evenly across the soil. A small stream may
have flowed through here long ago. The dale is also very damp, probably due to
all of the shade caused by the surrounding walls of earth.
name: Rocky Dale

desc:
The dale stretches across from wall to wall by a good twenty feet, one would
guess. The walls on both the west and east side are solid earth, but a few stray
roots have emerged from them. Along the top of the ledges, tall grass can be
noticed, with each blade dancing back and forth. The dale becomes more narrow to
the north, and seems to become wider as it continues south from here. The soil
along the ground is damp, probably from all the shade.
name: Narrowing Dale

desc:
The dale is very narrow here, ranging between ten to twelve feet across. It
becomes even more narrow to the north, but expands in width to the south. It is
very damp here, due to the high walls of earth blocking sunlight on both the
western and eastern side. It would appear that this used to be some kind of a
channel or a small stream that flowed through here, because the ground is evenly
worn. High above the walls, a grassy ledge is noticeable, and it looks as though
some tall lengths of grass sway in formation there.
name: Narrow Dale

desc:
Long blades of green grass consume the meadow here. Some tall weeds are seen
scattered across the meadow, surpassing even the tallest blades of grass. There
are many stones spread out among the meadow as well, some being more noticeable
than others. The meadow appears to expand to the west and to the north, while a
small pond is clearly noticeable to the south. Though this part of the meadow is
mostly flat, it appears to end abruptly to the east, where there is a drop down
to what seems like a small valley or dale below.
name: Grassy Meadow

desc:
The grasses grow here without trouble it would appear. Some stalks have reached
nearly the height of a full-grown man, but numerous brown stalks also litter the
ground. Perhaps after certain seasons they wither, and then begin to grow again
after.
name: Tall Grasses

desc:
The fields part here, revealing a clearing covered with dust. The reddish hue of
the dirt means that the soil must contain some red clay. The clay might not
provide ample nutrients, since there are only a few withered weeds at this
clearing. A few cracks can be noticed within the ground also, probably due to
lack of water. The fields which surround the clearing appear to be more fertile
and green.
name: Clearing in the Field

desc:
Oak and elm trees inhabit this part of the forest, and a thick carpet of fallen
leaves from autumns past covers the ground. Dense groups of hawthorn bushes grow
along the riverside, the river to the south and to the west provides a ample
supply of water the plants need to flourish. There is a sturdy pier on the river
facing towards the west. A thick rope is running across the water towards the
other side of the river.
name: Forest of Oaks

desc:
A pine tree, nearly five feet in diameter, is blocking the path to the north.
The height of this tree appears impossible to estimate, as it seems to nearly
touch the sky. Streaks of sap along the trunk of the pine tree show that while
it has stood here for a long time, it is still full of life.
name: Beneath a Tall Pine Tree

desc:
The branch of this pine tree is large enough to support the weight of several
men, but the ends of the limb look too precarious to dare. A number of skitters
and scuttles suggest that some woodland creatures call this lofty place home.
name: On a Bough of a Pine Tree

desc:
The ground is very flat across the plain here, with few plants other than the
ubiquitous grass. Even though the plains are completely covered with it, it is
quite short since the trees to the west seem to absorb most of the moisture.
Small purple wildflowers can also be seen jutting out of some patches of grass.
name: Flat Plains

desc:
This area is rife with thick stalks of grass and large weeds. The soil here must
contain an abundance of nutrients, while the plains south of here look very flat
and barren. The minute slivers of rotting wood that are on the ground here may
have something to do with the robust plant life.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
In the southern half of the plain stands a large boulder. Surrounding this
boulder are many stones of similar texture, but they range differently in their
sizes. The boulder has a unique shade of grey, almost metallic under certain
lighting. Grass has grown around the odd boulder, being very thick near the
soil, but as it reaches upward it begins to slump. The wide plain stretches to
the east from the boulder.
name: Rocky Plain

desc:
The grass is very long and full in the meadow. Most of the grass is a colour of
green, but a few dead blades of grass remain a light shade of brown and appear
very crisp. A fading brushwood can clearly be seen to the west. Some of the
debris from the brushwood has made its way here to the meadow, swept along by
the wind.
name: Grassy Meadow

desc:
An oval-shaped pond lies in the middle of the meadow, though along the western
side of it is the slope of a plateau. Along the northern half of the pond stand
truly long blades of grass, weeds and other vegetation that have grown out from
the water. Though most of these plants within the pond are green, the lower
portions of their stems are brown where they have idled in the water for too
long. Small pebbles sit beneath the water, adding more colour to the pond.
name: A Pond in the Meadow

desc:
From this point in the grassy meadow, an oval shaped pond is in sight to the far
south. Grass of varying textures and sizes covers the meadow together with a few
weeds. Small thistle bushes sit widely apart from one another, nestled in among
the longer blades of grass. The meadow continues to expand to the east and
south.
name: Meadow near a Pond

desc:
Thorny weeds and wide brambles are vigorously prospering amidst a dense blanket
of heather shrubs. The ground is pretty uneven with sudden holes concealed under
the heather plants, and large, white rocks rest on a herbal pedestal. The sounds
of many tiny animals can be heard nearly all the time, busily scurrying around.
name: Thorny Heather Field

desc:
Dense and crowded, a sheer endless number of rhododendrons populates the plains.
Green, short grass filled with dandelions forms a dense underbrush together with
on the first glance inseparable heather, making travelling quite hard with only
a few paths in it. Rarely, a short, narrow pine juts out of the wilderness, the
lower parts of it concealed by the rhododendrons.
name: Rhododendron-covered Lowlands

desc:
On this large meadow, many clusters of wild flowers can be seen. All around,
patches of them grow in all colours and sizes. Deep in the grass and the
clusters, rustling of vermin or similar beasts can be heard, and a faint humming
hints at all kinds of insects. Some clusters have had their time of prospering
in this season, while other plants have not yet matured and are still waiting
for their time. To the east, the meadows suddenly end. A deep abyss stretches
from the north to the south and it is not possible to see its bottom.
name: Clusters of Wild Flowers

desc:
Hazel shrubs are rather common here, spread over the bed of grass and only
rarely clustered, yet numerous. They grow more densely in the southern part of
these plains where the ground seems to be wetter, and the grass even greener.
Numerous obvious tracks of small animals can be noticed especially in the close
vicinity of the hazel shrubs.
name: Hazels in the Plains

desc:
Bubbling out of the ground, water flows out of many tiny and a few major
springs, joining up to a small stream and watering the surrounding muddy plains.
Grass grows high and wildly in the proximity, and hop and cattail sprawls
untamedly near the springs. Fungi, moss and a few solitary overhanging weeding
willows round up the impression of a very lively vegetation.
name: Source of a Small Stream

desc:
A tiny stream meanders through the meadows here, slowly flowing in a more or
less curvy way from the north and then bending westwards. A few distant trees
are scattered over the plains, looming out of the high-growing golden grass that
populates it. A few clusters of poppies add strong red dots to the colour of the
plains when blossoming.
name: Small Stream in the Plains

desc:
What brought this huge boulder here cannot be said. It lies within a low meadow,
and all around, herbs and some wild flowers grow. To the west, a deep abyss
formed a long time ago, and the plain suddenly vanishes into the darkness. Apart
from the boulder, only a few bushes arise above the grass and the flowers.
name: A Huge Boulder in the Plains

desc:
The intense fragrance of herbs growing wildly in the plains fills the air,
adding a fresh and spicy note to it. Only rarely, grass manages to grow between
the spices, being nearly completely crowded out by them. The ground looks rather
rich of humus, being able to save water over a long time, feeding the plants
plentifully even in drier times.
name: Natural Herb-garden

desc:
On a gold-filled and green meadow, low-hanging and cantilevered weeping willows
stand far from each other, giving anything hiding under them rather good cover
from eyesight. The ground is smooth and flat with occasional dents and looks as
if it stretched for miles. Rather often, noise can be heard in the grass,
indicating a lot of busy animals hiding in it.
name: Scattered Willows

desc:
Embedded and sheltered by seemingly endless golden and yellow grass, manifold
flowers prosper among it. During their bloom, the area is coloured with blossoms
in all colours of the rainbow, changing its face every day. Rarely, a solitary
tree can be seen, its root partially hidden behind the plants. Occasionally, the
wind carries the sound of flowing water with it from the west.
name: Flower-filled Plains

desc:
Poppies and cornflowers are widespread among fields of golden and green grass,
painting the area in lucent red and bright blue during most times of the year.
In some places the flowers grow so dense that it seems impossible to distinguish
them from others. The ground which is only barely discernable under the
vegetation is reddish-brown, containing clumps of clay.
name: Field of Poppies

desc:
Short and low grass and hard, sturdy weeds grow here in thick, interwoven
clusters. While occasionally a bush grows here and there breaking up the dense
vegetation, the stony, dark soil is clearly visible at other places. Grey
boulders and smaller stones rest among the grass, partially protected against
the common wind.
name: Weed-filled Grassland

desc:
The long blades of grass from the meadow to the west become quite shorter in
length here as the plain stretches eastward. The wide plain grows many weeds,
all scattered around in small bundles. The grass is very coarse and it is not
covering the ground in some areas. There are several patches of dirt visible
around the plain.
name: Open Plain

desc:
The open plain is very bare with little to notice other than the fine growing
grass. The grass is very thin, and looks quite refined. The plain continues to
the north while to the west some water is visible. Dry patches of soil begin at
the edge of the grass to the south, where it appears to be even more desolate.
name: Open Plain

desc:
The bare plain is covered with small patches of coarse grass and some weeds that
are very tall and of a bright green colour. The patches of grass are
contrastingly dark in colour, and also add a touch of brown to the vegetation.
The ground appears rough where it is bare of grass, giving the plain a very
desolate look; there are some dirt plots in the area that the weeds have not
conquered yet.
name: Bare Plain

desc:
The low country around here is all dry. Small hummocks of dried grass colour the
landscape with their variety of hues. Between all those hummocks some sand can
be noticed, ready to consume all moisture that any possible rain might give. The
grains of sand appear to be very fine and evenly spread out. Some deadwood
breaks through the tussocks here.
name: Dry Meadow

desc:
Rocks are spread out along the plain in various sizes and colours. There are
some patches of rough-looking grass that are filled with overgrown weeds as they
battle for dominance over the plain. A few tiny pebbles lie among the dirt in
places where the grass has not grown for awhile.
name: Rocky Plain

desc:
Countless boulders are strewn across the plain. Not very many blades of grass
are visible, only some rough-looking weeds stand tall. There are several tiny
stones mixed into the large areas of bare dirt. The stones are all dark-looking
and blend in well with the soil. To the west, a lot more vegetation is
distinguishable in various shades of green.
name: Barren Plain

desc:
The grass has grown quite fine here, bright green in colour and very thick in
appearance. The grass ends abruptly to the south, where there is a long drop
down to an old streambed below. The stump of an old tree stands firmly in the
earth still, with a few flowers growing out from around it. The plains appear to
expand to the east, and the ledge continues to the west.
name: Ledge of Fine Grass

desc:
The rolling country here is covered by a thick grassbed. Some small herbs can be
found growing amongst the regular vegetation of these grassy plains. A weak maze
of animal trails can be seen in the grass. The ground becomes bare of grass,
with only a few weeds noticeable, towards the western side of the plain.
name: Grassy Plain

desc:
The broken country here is growing weak and dry grass, which is both brown and
green in colour. Occasional stumps of deadwood are scattered around, half-rotten
and half-buried into the ground. A lonely skeletal form of a long dead bovine is
lying here, almost covered by a hummock of exceptionally strong grass.
name: Dried Plain

desc:
Various stumps and boles of long dead trees are lying around here. On a few of
the stumps some scar-like markings can be seen, probably cut by a crude hatchet
or axe. Now some thick and dry brushwood dominates this area, covering
everything and choking back the trees' every attempt to regain their former
glory.
name: Remnants of a Forest

desc:
The ground here is barren and dark, being coloured by shades of black and brown.
Most of the ground is dry, so dry it appears like concrete and is filled with
small hollows and cracks. Small patches of dry grass and various half-dead
plants are scattered among the ground.
name: Sere Ground

desc:
Several weeds have sprouted from beneath the ground here, mixed in among long
blades of grass. The grass appears to be very crisp and dried. There are some
flowers growing along the western edge of the field, where a narrow dale courses
through the landscape. It looks like a dried-up streambed. The furrows in the
ground suggest that the water once flowed in from the east, perhaps creating a
small waterfall at this point.
name: At the Edge of a Narrow Dale

desc:
The field grows long blades of grass spreading out evenly across the ground.
Between them are patches of dried and bare ground. The grassy ledge, which is
almost unnoticeable until it is too late, travels along the western side of the
field, completely covered by tall lengths of grass and weeds. There are a few
stones littered about the field as well.
name: Grassy Ledge

desc:
The grass is fine here, bright green in colour, with only a few weeds noticeable
between the slender blades. Four trees have also grown here, spreading out
several feet from one another, all having long and thin branches. The leaves on
these trees are small, green, and oval in shape. The bark upon the trees appears
to be very smooth and light brown in colour. The grass gets taller as it expands
to the east, where there is also a small mound noticeable in the distance.
name: Slightly Wooded Meadow

desc:
The grassy meadow continues in several directions here; north, west and south.
To the east, there is a drop down to the ground below, to the bottom of a valley
or a small dale that exists there. The grass is very soft and bright-coloured
green at the edge of the drop, indicating that the soil must be very fertile
there. Occasional weeds can be noticed throughout the meadow, but all of them
are of the same kind, with spear-shaped leaves and dark green colour.
name: Grassy Meadow

desc:
The meadow here consists of mainly bright green grass that appears very young
and healthy. On the western side of the meadow, there are a few weeds, and the
grass is much thicker and more dried. The grassy meadow suddenly ends on the
eastern side, where there is a long drop to the dale below. Tall grass has grown
on the ledge, almost hiding it from view.
name: Ledge by a Grassy Meadow

desc:
The meadow is covered by soft grass that is the colour of bright green. The soil
must be very fertile here, as the grass appears to be so healthy. To the east,
the meadow suddenly ends, and a grassy ledge can be noticed bordering a narrow
dale. Along the ledge, some of the bare earth is revealed, and the grass there
grows wild and long compared to the grass of the delicate meadow.
name: Ledge by a Meadow

desc:
Tall but strong grass dominates this very flat field. This goes to show the
extreme lushness of the ground, though it is choking back every other possible
plant form which might sprout here. Not even a bush or a small tree breaks the
monotony of this grassland.
name: Flat Sea of Grass

desc:
The ground is covered by a thick bed of grass, with very few noticeable weeds.
To the east the grass becomes longer, though the growth is not as thick as it is
here. Some small pebbles are also noticeable along the east side of this grassy
area. This appears to be a ledge, since to the west and south there is a great
distance down to the ground below.
name: Grassy Ledge

desc:
Tall yet dry grass dominates the area here. Though suffering from aridity, the
grass still shows the fertility of the ground. Some weak trails can be spotted
in the thick carpet, revealing the existence of animal life in these fields. The
grass meets a dale running through the lands in the south, where the roof of a
wooden structure can be noticed below.
name: Dried Sea of Grass

desc:
The channel is completely dry, filled by tiny grains of sand and small pebbles.
It looks as though water had at one time flowed southwest from here, where the
sand slowly begins to fade and small spots of grass can be noticed. On one side
of the dried-up channel stands a tall tree, coated by a grey-coloured bark. A
few flowers have sprouted up from the earth on both sides of the sandy bed
together with several stones. A large hill stands to the east, but due to the
erosion over the years, it is impossible to reach it from this side.
name: Dried-up Channel

desc:
The barren, sandy landscape here is dominated by a dried, meandering channel,
which still bears visible marks of once flowing water. Pebbles along the dry
valley are round, as the once gushing water has curved the edges from all
stones. Small bushes have surrounded the channel and dried there in the past,
leaving only empty skeletal branches behind. The channel continues to both the
north and the south.
name: Dead Channel

desc:
A few leafless trees are scattered around. Their naked branches hang in the
moist air, weighed down by the silence. There is little evidence of wildlife on
the ground, but now and again you hear the sounds made by some creatures in the
trees. The ground begins to slope down to the east, where you catch the sound of
water trickling down the side of the valley.
name: Thin Forest

desc:
Here barren trees stand quietly, isolated in pools of mist. The ground is soft,
sticky in places, with soil that seems barely able to sustain life. The damp air
is still and smells musty and unhealthy. The silence is only broken by the odd
crack of a decaying branch. The surface under foot deteriorates preventing any
further progress to the south and west.
name: Dying Forest

desc:
There is a dense forest of birches, elms, and oaks all around. The tops of the
trees are not visible due to the thick canopy of leaves and branches above. The
canopy is so thick that the rays from the sun cannot reach the undergrowth of
this forest.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
The leafless trees cling to the sides of the valley that slopes sharply down to
the northeast. The air smells of damp or rotting wood. The interconnection of
the tree roots makes it impossible to ride here. From the vantage point of a
raised rocky outcrop one can make out the glitter of the great river Anduin in
the distance.
name: Valley Side

desc:
The ground climbs steeply to the south. The soil is soft and there is much
evidence of mudslides. Many trees have failed in their battle to grow on these
slopes. The few trees that survived sport moss-covered roots and have beards of
vegetation hanging from their branches. The noise of a river is carried from the
north on a breeze.
name: Forest by a River

desc:
Only medium and small trees grow in this section of the forest. The logs and
cracked stumps here must belong to larger trees which eventually failed to
maintain a hold of the root-filled earth. The soil seems rich in nutrients
brought downstream by the river, and the ground has become very wet.
name: Small Grove of Trees by the Riverbank

desc:
The Anduin river to the east has separated this forest of oaks and pine trees
from the wide grasslands that spread out across the other side. The dense and
leafy canopy created by the criss-crossing tree branches looks as if it would
block out any form of outside light. The river appears to be flowing to the
south.
name: Dense Forest near a River Bend

desc:
A few huge oaks and large sycamores are all that reside in this area of the
forest. The closer to the river, the more lush the vegetation becomes, until
some of it becomes submerged, yet thriving still. The river flows strongly on
from the north to the east past the riverbank. A thick rope is running across
the water towards the other side of the river.
name: Light Forest on the Riverbank

desc:
The cloudy waters continue to move downstream. A variety of small pebbles, dead
leaves, and broken twigs are being washed along in the moving current. Their
turbulent motion in the water serves as a reminder of the forces of nature. The
river seems to be gradually bending eastwards.
name: Anduin River

desc:
The river continues to flow here, with some leafy trees on both banks. These
trees are bent slightly towards the water, in order to get a better amount of
sunlight. It is almost as though they were bowing to any travellers who make
their way down the river.
name: Anduin River Bend

desc:
Small rocks tumble down the fast-flowing river that cuts through the bend here.
The jagged edges of the rocks are slowly becoming smooth, as they collide with
other rocks in the deep river. Perhaps these rocks are clastic sediment that has
broken away from the mountains far to the west. The river bends to the east.
name: Fast Moving Bend in the Anduin

desc:
This section of the Anduin River narrows as it goes from the north to the south.
On both sides of the river, ancient forests reside, uncaring of the war that
consumes Middle-earth. The current of the river is slow, but it is powerful and
constant.
name: Anduin River

desc:
The current of the Anduin does not stop or falter along its ongoing course to
the southeast. A light forest of oaks and elms blocks all view to the north,
while to the east there is a muddy riverbank and an expanse of plains. The water
continues to rush onwards, carrying an occasional piece of wood or debris along
with it as it flows to the south.
name: Anduin River Bend

desc:
The current changes its direction to head south from here. Leaves from all kinds
of trees can be seen tumbling in the water. There are also the submerged husks
of uprooted trees residing on the bottom of the river, waiting to surprise any
traveller on the water with an errant branch.
name: Bend in the River Anduin

desc:
A noticeable natural track that has developed from animals wandering the same
way for years is winding through lush grass. A few bushes covered with dusty red
berries line up the borders of the path, while scattered among the plains, more
bushes are visible. In the south-western part of the area, the path widens and
leads to a bubbling spring.
name: Animal Path by a Spring

desc:
The air is filled with soothing scents of herbs and plants that are spread
throughout the fields. Wherever no herb grows, golden grass has claimed its
share of the ground. Veins of clay can be noticed on the earth, being hardened
and cracked and forming a solid layer over the more fertile bed. Although much
of the rain drains off over the surface, it still seems to be sufficient to feed
the well-adjusted vegetation.
name: Grasslands

desc:
The ground here serves as a home for plants of a great variety in height and
shape. The dominating golden grass shifts in colour from its natural royal tone
over yellow to grey, and in some smaller areas, it is even spotted with tender
purple traces. Other plants are growing allotted among the grass like small
colourful dots.
name: Grass-filled Lowlands

desc:
Row upon row of tall and proud golden hay stands in the field. In this field,
moisture seems to be available in abundance, allowing the hay to grow at its
peaks to the size of a small man. Activity of busy insects is heard through the
stems, and from time to time their noise becomes loud and angry.
name: Lush Grass

desc:
Overlapping turfs of golden hay are covering the fat and rich earth, mostly low
and wide. A few patches of grass among them reach out for the sky, growing to
the height of a man's waist with bulbous heads swaying in occasional breezes.
Coming from the east, a fragrance of wood and resin fills the air, although it
seems to be quite a distance away.
name: Golden Turf

desc:
The wind-beaten land is scarcely covered with grass. The turfs seem to be
clustered, and dirt and gravel cover the ground between the coasts of grass.
Where the wind has been able to work freely, on small rises and inclines, grey
rocks sticking out from beneath the soil have been left naked to the elements.
name: Sparse Grassland

desc:
Small bunches of dried grass colour this otherwise pebbly and gravelled area of
the plain. The dullness of the surroundings forces even the most persistent life
forms to seek cover and rest from other places, though by the traces on the
ground they appear to at least travel through.
name: Dying Grass

desc:
The scenery here is flat, slightly rising towards the east. The ground is barren
and only some persistent grass manages to find its living here. Where the grass
grows weak, a rocky ground peeks from below. Some bare land can be spotted
around as well, eroded by heavy rains in the past. The thin, struggling grass
appears to be growing stronger and taller to the south.
name: Rocky Slope

desc:
Tall grass and wild cereals cover the ground, bending and rustling under gusts
of wind. Here and there, flowers protrude from the grass, sparkling when lit by
the sun. Noises of rodents darting and cheeping around can be heard rather
often, and clear imprints on the dark, soft soil indicate that game seems to be
common here.
name: High Grass

desc:
This deep, dry basin seems completely lifeless. The ground is dry and extremely
hard-surfaced, coloured with shades of black and brown. The brinks of this bowl
do not rise very steeply. Along the northern edge of the old pool huge logs and
small boulders can be noticed, perhaps the remains of an ancient pier.
name: Extinct Lake

desc:
The slightly rolling country has suffered badly from continuous erosion, caused
by aridity, heat and time. The pebbly and sandy ground here grows only some weak
grass but incredibly lush bushes, which reveal some of the landscapes former
fertility. Some ancient formations of stone can be seen around, half buried into
the sand and bush growth.
name: Lush Bushes

desc:
The landscape here is barren, growing weak grass and various thorny bushes. The
ground is mostly composed of sand and pebble instead of soil. Some man-sized
boulders are lying here, half buried in the sand, and branches of thorny growth
are rising out from their sides. To the west, the scenery rises slightly while
it becomes more rocky.
name: Thorny Bushes

desc:
A small cliff rises from the ground here, covered by boulders of various sizes.
The bald ledge points northwards, offering no base for any growth. Chutes of
esker lead in all other directions. In the middle of the cliff there seems to be
a boulder-covered crevice.
name: Highland Boulders

desc:
A small depression opens here in the middle of the pebbly fields. Southwards, a
steep slope has formed a natural shelter against the elements. A small trickle
of water can be found flowing in from the east and into the depression, only to
disappear below one large boulder that is standing there alone.
name: Rocky Depression

desc:
The growth of tall grass dominates the flat ground here. A small spring gushes
here, forming a small stream which then flows rapidly to the west, giving some
much needed moisture to all vegetation. Some blooming plants colour the land
which surrounds the tiny source of the brook. This paradisiacal oasis does not
seem to have any larger plants though.
name: Tall Grass

desc:
A short but strong grassbed covers the ground here. The surroundings are painted
in various shades of green. A constant wind may have forced most of the stalks
of grass to bend low in this open area. Some small plants have grown at random
spots here and there, wherever they could manage to find shelter against the
weather.
name: Short Grass

desc:
Thin bushes rise from the rocky soil here, covering almost all of the ground
with their growth. More than half of the vegetation is dried and extremely close
to death. The rest of the bushes still try to grow some weak leaves, which are
dehydrated by aridity and sunshine. A few stones are scattered here along the
ground, smashed into the dry earth.
name: Dying Bushes

desc:
Some stunted and slow-growing bushes rule the landscape here. Weak grass and
some especially strong plants grow below the bushes. The undergrowth is formed
mostly of different waxy-leafed herbs, which can accept this barren plain as
their substrate.
name: Stunted Plain

desc:
Small bushes have spread their growth all around this area, leaving all of the
grass as a weak minority which is forced to fight for its existence. The
alteration of different ground types is clearly visible here, when the lush
steppes, the arid field and the scrubby plains meet. Only a few weeds appear to
stand out, mainly due to their dark colour.
name: Small Bushes

desc:
Bushes and shrubs rule the ground, spreading more in width than in height,
adapting in shape and colour to the underlying earth. A few turfs of golden
grass sway by their stems, and a scarce supply of herbs stands rebellious
against the dominating vegetation, struggling not to get crowded out.
name: Meandering Brush

desc:
In the air here, there flows a gentle fragrance of wood and resin, generated by
low, woody shrubs covering the ground. Where bushes and twigs have lost their
grasp on the land, dirt and grass cover the earthy ground, here and there
adjourned by clusters of dandelions and moss. Chickweed seems to grow
everywhere.
name: Rosiny Bushes

desc:
Short but wide shrubs nestle to each other, forming a dense, green and brown
living carpet which burdens movement. Only at a few spots, the carpet opens for
some kind of natural pathways paved with nettles, thistles and other small
plants. The fragrance of different herbs can be smelled in the air, mingled with
a faint, yet sharp stench.
name: Squat Bushes

desc:
Shimmering stems of grass growing in colonies spread out all over the dark,
earthy ground. A few herbs grow among them, and a bush here and a shrub there
are the only things disturbing their dominance. The air is filled with the smell
of the grass, making it fresh and spicy.
name: Golden Grasses

desc:
The field is covered with thin, woody grass. A few flowers manage to survive
while being at a permanent strife for survival. Rocks lie here and there on the
ground, and close to most of them, bushes and higher grass grow. The soil is
hard and poor, and dust can be felt in the air.
name: Scarce Grassland

desc:
Among fields of grass in tones of gold and yellow, a tiny hill arises,
overlooking the vicinity. Although being only small in size, the hill is still
noticeable compared to the flat surrounding ground. Green grass and moss cover
it, and a few barren spots can be seen on it. Flowers of all colours grow
everywhere, making this a good landmark for a traveller.
name: A Tiny Hill

desc:
The rank ground seems to have hardened over time here. Fragile stems of grass in
gold and yellow and cabbages in all tones of brown and green cover it. Gravel,
rocks and hard chunks of soil form the surface between the strays of grass.
Small patches of moss clutch at the earth, slowly easing the ground again.
name: A Dry Field

desc:
Clusters of grass silently sway as if troubled by their state, fighting for
existence on the meagre, yet wet soil. Thorny weeds, thistles and other haulms
fill the large gaps between the grass turfs, seemingly growing out of the grass.
Gravel accompanies the lowest straws and cabbages on the ground.
name: Thorny Grassland

desc:
Tall and proud grass grows here in abundance, dominating the ground and rising
to the waist of a man, confident in its becoming. Its edges are pointy, tickling
a wanderer with every move, while small plants of many different kinds grow
among it. A few rocks lie around, being a disruption of the otherwise perfectly
level landscape.
name: Rich Grass

desc:
A low ledge cuts through the land. Tall and golden grass grows throughout the
area, bathing the surrounding ground in gold and green. Its cause is uncertain,
but it has left a glaring scar in the fertile landscape, and grass and small
plants can only cover the edge. Rocks and gravel have tumbled down and lie in a
stony field to the north below the ledge.
name: At a Ledge

desc:
A rocky ledge cuts the flat and even landscape like a scar, separating the lower
ground to the north from the higher one to the south of it. High grass grows on
the higher side of the ledge and hangs over its sharp edge, smoothening its
appearance. Among the grass, many different flowers paint the landscape with
their natural, colourful ink.
name: A Rocky Ledge

desc:
The soil here has a genuine richness that would make any attempt at farming a
success. The grass of these fields, uninhibited by poor soil or lacklustre
weather, have covered the entire landscape with their golden threads, growing
tall and strong. Among them, other weeds can be spotted only very rarely, and
wherever they strive for survival, the grass seems to overwhelm it.
name: Thriving Grass

desc:
To the south, the way is blocked by a wide ledge, crossing through the fields.
In the shadows beneath, there it lie rocks and gravel as if hurled from the edge
above. Traces of animal activity can be seen all along the wall as this location
is perfect for protection from the path of the sun in the south. A few dark
spots can be seen along the rock of the ledge.
name: Below a Ledge

desc:
Golden and green grass covers the rich ground, growing only small in height, yet
flowing in every breeze of wind, bowing to it in wandering waves. To the south,
a ledge runs through the land, looming up over the flat fields. On top of it,
more of the golden grass grows in abundance and high in size, flowing over the
ledge. In a small stony circle formed by a few well placed rocks right beneath
the ledge, a small pool of water has gathered.
name: Beneath a Ledge

desc:
Small dunes and some bumps cross through this area, mostly in the southern part
of it. On the higher parts of the ground, it seems to be drier. Only some golden
grass grows there that has partially turned grey and brown. In the lower and
wetter areas, the grass grows stronger with a more luscious tone, and large
shrubs spread their branches wide and high.
name: Uneven Plains

desc:
A thin layer of top-soil tries to cover the ground here, but in some places, it
has been eroded and reveals a thick layer of granite. Grass can only be seen in
a few places among large areas of ling, and a tiny path leads northwards sloping
between a few hills. Rocks of all shapes and sizes rest upon the path from their
brief fall down the nearby elevations.
name: Rocky Ground

desc:
Although the river is wide, the water here is deep and hurries between the high
banks. This is truly a great river. In places the long thirsty roots of the bare
trees dip into the river providing you with a chance of scrambling up the bank.
The forests on the banks make it impossible to get a view of the lands to either
side. The trees to the southeast seem to be thinner. Only the gurgle of the
river swirling among the roots breaks the silence.
name: River Anduin

desc:
The river widens and turns southwards here. Tall thin trees on the steep banks
to the north and east block the view of the lands beyond. The air is fresh and
clean, reminiscent of the springtime. From time to time bubbles break the
surface of the water as fishes come up to gulp some air or snatch an insect.
name: River Bend

desc:
High grass grows all over in this cave, and the rocky walls are also covered
with many thick, green tendrils. Where no other plant could take root, soft and
shimmering moss covers the grey rock. Near the southern wall, clear water flows
from a small spring and vanishes between some cracks in the rock.
name: The Overgrown Cave

desc:
This cavern appears to be home to various small insects. Here and there in the
dirt, a dead beetle or a squirming maggot can be noticed. The dirty cavern
descends further, but one could only guess what is at its end. There is a tiny
animal skull lying on the floor of the cavern, stripped completely bare.
name: Small Cavern

desc:
Silence hangs in this cave like an ever present gloom as all noise seems muffled
and absorbed by the earthen walls. No draft of fresh air is felt in this dark
place, yet a passage seems to ascend towards the surface through another,
smaller cavern. Flecks of stripped bone dot this cave's dirt floor like mosaic
tiling.
name: Dark Cave

desc:
The evident lack of nutrients has left the shrubs weak and twisted at this
place. On the ground, more rocks than soil can be seen, and the fine layer of
dust that lies atop shifts in occasional breezes. A low, lonely tree can be seen
on a small incline. Large rocks surround it in a nearly perfect semicircular
shape as if specifically placed there, and surprisingly dense bushes grow close
by.
name: Twisted Brush

desc:
Several planks of mallorn wood have been fashioned into a long and wide vessel,
slightly curved and with a flat bottom. At the side of the ferry there is a
large, robust-looking wheel mechanism connected to a thick rope that stretches
all the way across the Anduin. Sturdy borders made of thick planks have been
erected at the edges of the ferry. A few boxes of cargo are tethered in the
middle, next to a post where mounts could be hitched to as well.
name: Ferry in the Anduin

desc:
The forest has many kinds of trees, all varying in their sizes. The trunks of
these trees are very large and the bark covering them is thick with different
textures. The undergrowth is very dense under the trees that are growing on the
eastern side. Large ferns stand out among all of the undergrowth, due to the
soft green colour that they have. A lot of moss can be seen growing thickly
along the ground here.
name: In the Forest

desc:
Thick, wet, and nutrient-rich from river runoff, the mud in this part of the
forest provides just enough anchorage to hold the roots of the oak trees that
grow here. Decomposing leaves and branches add to the richness of the soil,
providing a safe haven for swamp-dwelling grasses. To the north, the ground
becomes more stable and drier, and the thickness of the forest increases in that
direction as several trees block any further view.
name: Muddy Forest

desc:
Silent and veiled in an aura of tranquility, the chamber unveils wooden crates
adorned with delicate elven engravings. The walls breathe with tapestries
depicting woodland scenes, while the floor, fashioned from smoothly hewn stone,
carries the grace of Lothlorien craftsmanship. A huge piece of slate forms the
ceiling. At the north end is a staircase leading upwards through the slate.
name: Underground Supply Cache

desc:
You are in the middle of a patch of thorny brambles that grab at your arms and
legs as you move. Some black berries grow among the thorns. Small animals could
take refuge here with little change of being seen, let alone caught. You cannot
see far and have lost all sense of direction. The smell of animal droppings does
little to help you in this respect.
name: Patch of Brambles

desc:
Water flows slowly along a wide bend as the river changes its course from the
north to the east. While the flow is strong and the river is wide, the water
makes little sound. The northern bank is steep and high and it is impossible to
see what lies beyond it; only from the odd tree root protruding from the ground
one can gather some clues about the vegetation. The woodlands to the west
glitter with bright greens, golds and other light colours, giving the land a
look of springtime. You occasionally catch the sound of what seems to be
laughter carried on the breeze or perhaps just the chattering of birds.
name: Graceful Bend in the River
